questions.scenario: A developer is building a Claude-powered assistant and notices that important user preferences mentioned at the very beginning of a long conversation are being ignored later in the session.
questions.text: What is the PRIMARY architectural explanation for why Claude loses track of information placed in the middle of a long context window?

options.A: Claude's tokenizer strips repeated content after the first occurrence to save space.
options.B: Claude applies equal attention to every token in the context, so nothing is ever lost.
options.C: The 'lost in the middle' phenomenon means models attend less reliably to content far from the start or end of the context.
options.D: Claude automatically deletes content older than 10 turns to stay within the context window.

questions.explanation: Research into large language models has consistently shown that content positioned in the middle of a long context receives less reliable attention than content near the beginning or end. This 'lost in the middle' effect means that critical information should be anchored at the start or end of the context rather than buried in the middle of a long conversation.

whyWrongMap.A: The tokenizer does not deduplicate content; every token is encoded and passed to the model regardless of repetition.
whyWrongMap.B: Attention is not uniform across positions; empirical studies show middle-of-context recall degrades significantly as context length grows.
whyWrongMap.D: Claude does not have an automatic deletion mechanism; all tokens within the active context window are present, but attention distribution is non-uniform.

questions.scenario: A support agent built on Claude has been running for 40 turns. The operator wants to free up context space without losing the key issue the user is trying to resolve.
questions.text: Which built-in Claude Code mechanism is specifically designed to summarize the current conversation and free context space in a single step?

options.A: /clear
options.B: /compact
options.C: /summarize
options.D: /flush

questions.explanation: The /compact command in Claude Code generates a concise summary of the current conversation, replaces the full conversation history with that summary, and thereby frees a significant portion of the context window while preserving the essential narrative thread.

whyWrongMap.A: /clear erases the conversation entirely without producing a summary, resulting in complete loss of context rather than a condensed preservation of it.
whyWrongMap.C: /summarize is not an available Claude Code command; the correct command is /compact.
whyWrongMap.D: /flush is not an available Claude Code command; it is not part of the Claude Code CLI command set.

questions.scenario: An architect is designing a system prompt for a long-running coding assistant. The system prompt includes security constraints, the user's preferred programming language, and a lengthy history of past decisions.
questions.text: Where should the most safety-critical security constraints be placed within the context to ensure Claude reliably adheres to them?

options.A: Embedded within a large block of past decisions in the middle of the system prompt.
options.B: At the very start or very end of the system prompt, not buried in the middle.
options.C: In the first user turn rather than the system prompt.
options.D: Constraints do not need special placement because Claude attends equally to all positions.

questions.explanation: Due to the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon, content placed in the middle of long contexts receives less reliable attention. Safety-critical constraints should be anchored at the start or end of the system prompt so that they remain in the high-attention zones regardless of how long the conversation grows.

whyWrongMap.A: Embedding constraints inside a large block of historical decisions in the middle is precisely the anti-pattern the 'lost in the middle' effect warns against; those constraints are most likely to be under-attended.
whyWrongMap.C: System prompt is the correct location for persistent constraints; placing them in the first user turn exposes them to the same middle-of-context risk as the conversation grows and more turns are prepended or appended.
whyWrongMap.D: This contradicts established research; attention is not uniformly distributed across token positions, especially as context length increases.

questions.scenario: A tool used in an agentic pipeline returns a 50,000-token JSON dump of a database query result. The agent only needs three specific fields from that output.
questions.text: What is the recommended approach for handling this large tool output before passing it back into Claude's context?

options.A: Pass the full 50,000-token output unchanged so Claude can decide what is relevant.
options.B: Trim or filter the tool output server-side to include only the fields the agent actually needs before inserting it into the context.
options.C: Increase the model's context window by switching to a larger model tier.
options.D: Store the output in a vector database and never include it in the context.

questions.explanation: Large tool outputs should be trimmed before being inserted into the context. When only three fields are needed, including the full 50,000-token dump wastes context space, increases latency and cost, and pushes important earlier content further into the 'lost in the middle' zone. Server-side filtering to the relevant fields is the correct practice.

whyWrongMap.A: Passing the full output shifts the filtering burden onto Claude at inference time, consumes significant context budget unnecessarily, and may cause critical earlier content to fall into low-attention middle positions.
whyWrongMap.C: Switching to a larger context model treats a symptom rather than the cause; the output still contains 49,997 tokens of irrelevant data that should be removed regardless of model capacity.
whyWrongMap.D: Vector retrieval is a valid long-term memory strategy, but categorically never including tool output in the context prevents the model from reasoning over retrieved data in the current turn.

questions.scenario: A team runs a multi-session research assistant. After each session ends, the assistant produces a progressive summary of findings. In the third session, the team notices the assistant has forgotten a key constraint from session one that was only mentioned briefly in session one's summary.
questions.text: Which limitation of progressive summarization most likely caused this information loss?

options.A: Progressive summaries compress content each round, and details not reinforced in later sessions gradually drop out of the summary.
options.B: The summarization prompt used a temperature of 0, which prevents creative retention of edge-case details.
options.C: Claude's summarization always prioritizes the most recent turn over all previous turns.
options.D: Session boundaries automatically reset all prior constraints regardless of the summary strategy used.

questions.explanation: Progressive summarization involves compressing an already-compressed summary each session. Each compression round introduces information loss, and details that appear only briefly — especially constraints or edge cases — are disproportionately likely to be dropped when the model decides what is most salient. Critical constraints must be explicitly pinned or re-injected rather than relying on progressive compression to preserve them.

whyWrongMap.B: Temperature controls output randomness for generation tasks; it does not affect what semantic content a summarization model decides to retain or discard.
whyWrongMap.C: Claude does not have a hard-coded recency bias rule that always deprioritizes earlier content; the issue is the cumulative information loss inherent in repeated summarization.
whyWrongMap.D: Session boundaries are an application-layer concept; Claude itself has no mechanism that automatically resets constraints. The information loss is due to summarization compression, not a session reset.

questions.scenario: An operator is deciding between two context management strategies for a legal document review assistant: (A) run /compact every 20 turns, or (B) start a fresh conversation every 20 turns with no carry-over.
questions.text: When is starting a fresh conversation preferable to using /compact?

options.A: When the user's topic has fundamentally changed and earlier context provides no value or could mislead the model.
options.B: When the context window is less than 50% full, because /compact only activates when the window is nearly exhausted.
options.C: When the operator wants to preserve all prior decisions verbatim, because /compact deletes content permanently.
options.D: Fresh conversations are never preferable; /compact should always be used to maintain continuity.

questions.explanation: Starting fresh is preferable when the prior conversation history has no relevance to the new task or when stale context could introduce confusion or errors. /compact is best when continuity is needed but space is constrained. If the task domain has shifted entirely, carrying over an old summary adds noise rather than value, and a clean slate produces better results.

whyWrongMap.B: /compact can be invoked at any point regardless of how full the context window is; it is a deliberate operator or user action, not an automatic trigger tied to a capacity threshold.
whyWrongMap.C: /compact does produce a summary that replaces the prior history, but the purpose is compression not verbatim preservation; if verbatim preservation is required, the operator should store the full transcript externally before compacting.
whyWrongMap.D: Continuity through /compact is not universally superior; when prior context is irrelevant or misleading, a fresh conversation produces better results by removing the noise.

questions.scenario: A developer building a long-running coding assistant observes that after 30 turns, Claude starts violating a coding style rule (always use snake_case for variable names) that was stated in the system prompt. The system prompt is 8,000 tokens long with the style rule buried 4,000 tokens in.
questions.text: Which TWO actions, applied together, would most effectively prevent this regression?

options.A: Move the style rule to the beginning of the system prompt AND periodically re-state it at the end of the most recent assistant turn.
options.B: Increase the max_tokens parameter AND switch to a model with a larger context window.
options.C: Move the style rule to the end of every user turn AND disable the system prompt entirely.
options.D: Use /compact after each turn to keep the context short AND never include style rules in the system prompt.

questions.explanation: The 'lost in the middle' problem causes rules buried 4,000 tokens into an 8,000-token system prompt to receive less attention as the conversation grows. Moving the rule to the start of the system prompt places it in a high-attention zone. Periodically restating it at the end of the most recent turn keeps it in the recent high-attention zone as well. These two actions together reinforce the rule from both ends of the attention spectrum.

whyWrongMap.B: Increasing max_tokens and context window size does not address the attention distribution problem; a larger window means the middle zone is even larger and the buried rule is even less attended.
whyWrongMap.C: Disabling the system prompt removes all persistent instruction infrastructure; style rules belong in the system prompt, and appending them to every user turn is a workaround that clutters user messages and is not architecturally sound.
whyWrongMap.D: Running /compact after each turn is wasteful and destroys useful context prematurely; it does not solve the fundamental placement issue, and removing style rules from the system prompt is an anti-pattern.

questions.scenario: A team is building a customer service bot that handles hour-long sessions. They want to preserve the customer's issue description, any commitments the agent has made, and the list of troubleshooting steps already tried — without letting the context window fill up.
questions.text: Which context management strategy best balances preservation of critical information with efficient use of the context window?

options.A: Store critical fields (issue, commitments, tried steps) in a structured section at the start of the system prompt and refresh it after each turn; use /compact or summarization only for the conversational body.
options.B: Let the conversation grow unrestricted until the model raises a context length error, then start fresh.
options.C: Summarize the entire conversation including critical fields every five turns, discarding the original.
options.D: Keep only the last five turns in the context and discard everything older.

questions.explanation: Structured pinning separates critical, non-compressible data (issue, commitments, tried steps) from the conversational body that can tolerate summarization. Storing these fields in a structured block at the start of the system prompt and refreshing it ensures they remain in the high-attention zone and are never lost to progressive compression. The conversational body can then be summarized as needed without risking loss of the most critical information.

whyWrongMap.B: Waiting for a context length error is a reactive failure mode; it provides no graceful degradation and results in complete loss of session history.
whyWrongMap.C: Summarizing critical fields like commitments every five turns introduces progressive information loss; these fields should be pinned verbatim, not compressed.
whyWrongMap.D: Discarding all but the last five turns loses the issue description and the full list of tried steps, which are precisely the fields the team identified as critical.

questions.scenario: An agentic pipeline processes a sequence of tool calls. One tool returns a full web page HTML (120,000 characters). The next step only requires the page title and the first paragraph of body text.
questions.text: Beyond saving context space, what is an ADDITIONAL architectural benefit of trimming the tool output before injecting it into the context?

options.A: Trimmed outputs are automatically cached by Claude's prompt cache, while full outputs are not eligible for caching.
options.B: Trimming forces the model to attend to the relevant signal rather than potentially fixating on irrelevant parts of a large HTML document, improving reasoning accuracy.
options.C: Trimming increases the model's output token limit for the subsequent response.
options.D: Trimmed outputs bypass the safety classifier, reducing latency.

questions.explanation: Beyond context space savings, trimming large outputs improves reasoning quality. A 120,000-character HTML document contains navigation bars, scripts, ads, metadata, and other noise. Including this noise exposes the model to irrelevant tokens that can distract attention and degrade the quality of the downstream reasoning step. Providing only the title and first paragraph gives the model a focused, high-signal input.

whyWrongMap.A: Prompt cache eligibility is determined by whether content is in the system prompt prefix or stable conversation prefix, not by whether tool output was trimmed; trimming does not automatically confer cache eligibility.
whyWrongMap.C: The output token limit for a response is controlled by the max_tokens parameter and model capability; it is not affected by the size of tool output injected into the context.
whyWrongMap.D: Safety classifiers process all content regardless of output size; trimming does not bypass or alter safety evaluation pipelines.

questions.scenario: A financial compliance assistant runs 200-turn sessions. The system prompt contains: (1) regulatory rules (3,000 tokens at position 0), (2) a running log of user decisions (grows to 40,000 tokens in the middle), (3) a compliance checklist (2,000 tokens at the end). After 150 turns, auditors find Claude is inconsistently applying the regulatory rules but reliably following the checklist.
questions.text: Given the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon, what does this observation reveal about Claude's attention distribution, and what architectural change would address the regulatory rule inconsistency?

options.A: The observation is anomalous; Claude should attend equally to all positions, suggesting a model defect requiring vendor escalation.
options.B: The decision log has grown so large that it pushes the regulatory rules far into the middle of the effective context (below the 40,000-token body) despite their nominal position 0 in the system prompt, reducing their salience; the fix is to re-pin the regulatory rules at the end of the context after the decision log.
options.C: The regulatory rules at position 0 are actually in a low-attention zone; the checklist at the end receives high attention while position 0 receives the least; moving rules to the middle would fix this.
options.D: The checklist is short and simple, so Claude prefers it; the fix is to shorten the regulatory rules to match the checklist length.

questions.explanation: Although the regulatory rules are nominally at position 0 in the system prompt, the 40,000-token decision log inserted between them and the recent conversation effectively buries the rules deep in the middle of the total context the model processes. The checklist at the true end of the context remains in a high-attention zone and is reliably applied. The architectural fix is to re-anchor the regulatory rules at or near the end of the context — for example, by appending a concise regulatory summary as the last system-level block or at the end of each turn — so they remain in the high-attention tail zone.

whyWrongMap.A: Differential attention across positions is not a model defect; it is a well-documented property of transformer architectures. The observation is consistent with expected 'lost in the middle' behavior and should be addressed architecturally.
whyWrongMap.C: Position 0 (start of context) is a high-attention zone, not a low-attention zone. The problem is that the 40,000-token middle section effectively drowns the start, pushing attention toward only the very tail; the checklist benefits from being at the tail, not from being at the start.
whyWrongMap.D: Content length relative to a checklist is not the causal factor; the causal factor is positional attention distribution. Shortening the rules without addressing their positional relationship to the 40,000-token middle section would not reliably fix the inconsistency.

questions.scenario: An architect is designing a multi-session agent that performs ongoing scientific literature review. Each session ends with a progressive summary passed to the next session. After six sessions, the team discovers that an important methodological caveat noted in session one (that one key study used a non-standard sample population) has disappeared from the summary and is now absent from the agent's reasoning.
questions.text: Which combination of techniques would BEST prevent critical caveats from being lost across progressive summarization rounds?

options.A: Run /compact after every turn within each session to minimize the content that needs to be summarized at session end.
options.B: Maintain a separate structured 'critical facts' register outside the progressive summary that is explicitly preserved verbatim across sessions, and instruct the summarization step to never omit entries in that register.
options.C: Use a higher temperature when generating session summaries so the model explores more diverse retention strategies.
options.D: Switch to a model with a larger context window so that progressive summarization is never needed.

questions.explanation: The fundamental weakness of pure progressive summarization is that each compression round makes a salience judgment, and low-frequency but high-importance caveats are systematically under-prioritized. The architectural solution is to maintain a separate verbatim 'critical facts' register — a structured artifact that is never compressed and is explicitly carried forward each session. The summarization step for the narrative body is instructed to check and preserve all entries in this register. This hybrid approach protects rare, critical information from progressive information loss.

whyWrongMap.A: Running /compact within each session reduces intra-session context size but does not address the inter-session summarization problem; the session-end summary still compresses the full session narrative and will still drop low-salience caveats.
whyWrongMap.C: Higher temperature increases output variance but does not encode a systematic preference for retaining rare caveats; it is equally likely to introduce hallucinations as it is to retain the caveat, and is not a reliable preservation strategy.
whyWrongMap.D: A larger context window reduces how often summarization is needed but does not eliminate the need across six or more long sessions; the architectural weakness of pure progressive summarization remains, and the caveat loss would simply be deferred rather than prevented.

questions.scenario: An operator is evaluating three context management designs for a 500-turn enterprise workflow agent: Design X uses progressive summarization every 50 turns with no pinned register. Design Y uses a pinned structured register at the start of the context and summarization only for the narrative body. Design Z starts a fresh conversation every 50 turns with a manually curated handoff document.
questions.text: For a workflow where task continuity is critical, decisions accumulate over time, and rare edge-case constraints must never be lost, which design is architecturally superior and why?

options.A: Design X, because frequent summarization keeps the context small and prevents any 'lost in the middle' effect.
options.B: Design Z, because fresh conversations eliminate all attention distribution problems and the curated handoff ensures nothing is lost.
options.C: Design Y, because it separates compressible narrative context from non-compressible critical decisions, preserves rare constraints verbatim in the pinned register, and avoids the cumulative loss of progressive-only summarization.
options.D: All three designs are equivalent; context management strategy has no measurable effect on task performance over 500 turns.

questions.explanation: Design Y is architecturally superior for this specific scenario. It solves three distinct problems simultaneously: (1) the pinned register at the start of the context ensures rare edge-case constraints are never lost to compression; (2) summarization of only the narrative body limits compression to content that can tolerate it; (3) the pinned register also keeps critical data in a high-attention zone, mitigating the 'lost in the middle' problem. Design X loses edge cases through progressive compression. Design Z depends on the quality of a manual curation step, introduces discontinuities, and risks human error in the handoff document.

whyWrongMap.A: Design X's frequent summarization does keep the context small, but each compression round is a lossy operation for rare constraints. By turn 500 (ten compression cycles), edge-case constraints are highly likely to have been silently dropped.
whyWrongMap.B: Design Z introduces manual curation as a dependency; if the operator misses a constraint in the handoff document, it is permanently lost. Fresh conversations also lose the implicit context of accumulated decisions that inform later reasoning, introducing subtle discontinuities.
whyWrongMap.D: This is demonstrably false; the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon and progressive summarization information loss are empirically documented problems with measurable effects on task performance in long-horizon workflows.

questions.scenario: A senior architect must choose between three tool output handling strategies for an agent that runs 20 tool calls per session, each returning between 500 and 80,000 tokens: Strategy P — inject all tool outputs verbatim. Strategy Q — trim all outputs to 500 tokens regardless of content. Strategy R — apply content-aware trimming: extract only the fields the next reasoning step requires, preserve structure, and set a soft cap proportional to the tool's relevance score for the current task.
questions.text: Which strategy best balances context efficiency, reasoning quality, and preservation of critical information, and what is the key architectural principle it embodies?

options.A: Strategy P, because verbatim injection ensures no information is lost and Claude can filter internally.
options.B: Strategy Q, because a fixed 500-token cap guarantees predictable context usage and prevents any single tool from dominating the window.
options.C: Strategy R, because content-aware trimming applies the principle of minimum necessary context: inject precisely what the next reasoning step needs, no more, preserving signal density while preventing context bloat.
options.D: Strategy Q and Strategy P are equivalent in reasoning quality; only latency differs between them.

questions.explanation: Strategy R embodies the principle of minimum necessary context: provide exactly the information the next reasoning step requires and nothing more. This principle maximizes signal-to-noise ratio in the context, prevents high-volume tool outputs from pushing critical earlier content into the low-attention middle zone, and avoids the arbitrary truncation of Strategy Q which may cut relevant content. The relevance-score soft cap further adapts to the dynamic importance of each tool's output within the current task, rather than applying a context-agnostic fixed limit.

whyWrongMap.A: Strategy P assumes Claude can reliably filter internally, but verbatim injection of up to 80,000 tokens per tool call (20 calls = up to 1.6 million tokens) rapidly exhausts the context window and guarantees 'lost in the middle' degradation for earlier critical content. Internal filtering by the model is less reliable than upstream trimming.
whyWrongMap.B: Strategy Q's fixed 500-token cap is context-agnostic and will arbitrarily truncate outputs that contain critical information beyond token 500. A tool returning a 600-token JSON where the key field is at token 550 will silently lose that field, creating hard-to-diagnose reasoning errors.
whyWrongMap.D: Strategy Q (fixed 500-token truncation) and Strategy P (verbatim injection) differ significantly in reasoning quality: Q silently drops content beyond 500 tokens regardless of relevance, while P floods the context with noise. Neither is equivalent in quality to content-aware trimming.

questions.scenario: An AI assistant is helping a customer with a refund request. The customer's message is: 'I want my money back for the thing I bought last week.' The agent has no order history loaded in context.
questions.text: What is the most appropriate initial action for the agent?

options.A: Assume the customer means their most recent order and proceed with the refund.
options.B: Ask the customer to clarify which product or order they are referring to before proceeding.
options.C: Escalate immediately to a human agent because the request is ambiguous.
options.D: Deny the refund and ask the customer to call back with more information.

questions.explanation: When input is ambiguous and the missing information is easily obtainable from the user, the agent should ask a targeted clarifying question rather than assuming or escalating. Escalation is reserved for situations where the agent cannot resolve the ambiguity itself or where stakes are high enough to warrant human involvement.

whyWrongMap.A: Assuming the most recent order without verification risks processing the wrong refund, causing downstream errors and customer harm.
whyWrongMap.C: Immediate escalation for a simple clarifiable ambiguity wastes human agent capacity and degrades user experience when the agent could resolve it directly.
whyWrongMap.D: Denying a valid request due to missing context is unhelpful and damages trust; the correct response is to gather context, not refuse.

references: Anthropic Claude documentation: Handling ambiguous input
Task Statement 5.2: When to ask for clarification vs when to assume

questions.scenario: A customer support agent has a configured confidence threshold of 0.70. After retrieving policy information, the agent's internal confidence score for its answer is 0.55.
questions.text: Which escalation trigger has been activated?

options.A: Safety concern trigger
options.B: Conflicting information trigger
options.C: Confidence below threshold trigger
options.D: Ambiguous input trigger

questions.explanation: When an agent's confidence score falls below the configured threshold, it should activate the confidence-below-threshold escalation trigger. This is a distinct, quantitative signal separate from ambiguous input (which relates to the user's message) or conflicting information (which relates to data sources).

whyWrongMap.A: A safety concern trigger activates when content or actions pose potential harm, not when confidence is low on a factual answer.
whyWrongMap.B: Conflicting information trigger activates when two or more data sources provide contradictory facts, not simply when confidence is low.
whyWrongMap.D: Ambiguous input trigger relates to unclear or underspecified user requests, not to the agent's internal confidence in its retrieved answer.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Escalation triggers — confidence below threshold
Anthropic Claude documentation: Agent confidence and escalation

questions.scenario: A designer is building an escalation system and proposes using sentiment analysis to automatically route angry customers to human agents, arguing that negative sentiment is a reliable escalation signal.
questions.text: What is the primary problem with this approach?

options.A: Sentiment analysis is too expensive to run in real time.
options.B: Sentiment analysis is unreliable as an escalation signal; explicit signals should be used instead.
options.C: Negative sentiment always means the customer wants to cancel, not escalate.
options.D: Human agents should only handle positive-sentiment interactions.

questions.explanation: Sentiment analysis is considered unreliable for driving escalation decisions because it frequently misclassifies tone and does not capture the customer's actual intent or need. Explicit signals — such as the customer directly requesting a human, a defined keyword, or a rule-based trigger — are far more reliable escalation indicators.

whyWrongMap.A: Cost is not the primary concern; the fundamental issue is reliability, not latency or expense.
whyWrongMap.C: Negative sentiment has many interpretations; assuming it always signals cancellation intent is another form of the same unreliability problem.
whyWrongMap.D: This is incorrect; human agents handle a wide range of sentiment types, including frustrated, complex, and high-stakes interactions regardless of tone.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Sentiment analysis is unreliable for escalation; use explicit signals
Anthropic Claude documentation: Escalation design best practices

questions.scenario: An agent is processing a user request that requires a decision with potential legal implications. The agent cannot find a clear policy covering the situation and has no fallback knowledge base entry.
questions.text: Which escalation route is most appropriate?

options.A: Graceful degradation: inform the user the service is unavailable and end the session.
options.B: Route to a supervisor agent that has access to a broader policy knowledge base.
options.C: Attempt to infer the correct legal interpretation from context and proceed.
options.D: Ask the user to provide the relevant legal statute themselves.

questions.explanation: When an agent cannot handle a request — especially one with legal implications — and human review is not immediately available, routing to a supervisor agent with broader knowledge is the appropriate escalation path. The supervisor agent can either resolve the issue or escalate further to human review.

whyWrongMap.A: Graceful degradation is appropriate when no escalation path is available at all, not as a first-choice escalation route when a supervisor agent exists.
whyWrongMap.C: Inferring legal interpretations without a reliable basis creates compliance and liability risk; this should never be the chosen path for legal matters.
whyWrongMap.D: Asking a user to supply legal statutes is inappropriate; the system is responsible for its own knowledge gaps, not the user.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Escalation routing — human review, supervisor agent, fallback behavior
Anthropic Claude documentation: Multi-agent escalation patterns

questions.scenario: A customer has previously indicated in past sessions that they prefer self-service resolution and do not want to be transferred to a human agent unless absolutely necessary. Today they ask a complex billing question that falls just below the agent's confidence threshold.
questions.text: How should the customer preference tracking data influence the escalation decision?

options.A: Customer preferences should be ignored; confidence threshold rules always take precedence.
options.B: The agent should attempt a more thorough self-service resolution before triggering escalation, honoring the customer's documented preference.
options.C: The agent should immediately escalate because billing questions always require human review.
options.D: The agent should ask the customer to re-state their preference before deciding.

questions.explanation: Customer preference tracking is specifically intended to personalize escalation behavior. When a customer has documented a preference for self-service, the agent should honor that by attempting additional resolution steps — such as retrieving supplementary information or rephrasing the answer — before triggering escalation. The threshold is a guide, not an absolute override of documented customer preferences.

whyWrongMap.A: This ignores the purpose of preference tracking entirely; personalization is a first-class design concern in escalation systems.
whyWrongMap.C: Billing questions are not categorically escalation-required; complexity and confidence thresholds, combined with preferences, determine the appropriate path.
whyWrongMap.D: Re-asking for a preference that is already documented creates unnecessary friction and undermines the value of preference tracking.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Customer preference tracking for personalized escalation
Anthropic Claude documentation: Personalization in agent workflows

questions.scenario: An agent retrieves two internal documents about a return policy. Document A states returns are accepted within 30 days; Document B (newer) states returns are accepted within 14 days. The customer is asking whether their 20-day-old purchase is returnable.
questions.text: Which escalation trigger is active, and what is the correct handling approach?

options.A: Ambiguous input trigger; ask the customer to restate their question more clearly.
options.B: Conflicting information trigger; surface both policies to the customer and let them choose.
options.C: Conflicting information trigger; flag the conflict, do not commit to either policy, and route to human review or a supervisor agent.
options.D: Confidence below threshold trigger; answer with the more restrictive policy (14 days) to minimize business risk.

questions.explanation: Contradictory internal documents activate the conflicting information trigger. The agent should not arbitrarily choose one policy, as this could misrepresent the company's actual current policy and harm either the customer or the business. The correct action is to flag the conflict, withhold a definitive answer, and escalate to human review or a supervisor agent that can determine which document takes precedence.

whyWrongMap.A: The input is not ambiguous — the customer's question is clear. The problem is conflicting data sources, not the user's message.
whyWrongMap.B: Surfacing conflicting internal policy documents to customers is inappropriate; it exposes internal inconsistencies and forces a policy decision onto the customer.
whyWrongMap.D: Silently choosing the more restrictive policy is a unilateral policy decision by the agent, which it is not authorized to make; this bypasses proper escalation.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Escalation triggers — conflicting information
Anthropic Claude documentation: Handling conflicting data in agent responses

questions.scenario: An agent is mid-conversation when its escalation pathway to the human review queue becomes unavailable due to a system outage. A user is asking a question that would normally require human escalation.
questions.text: What is the correct graceful degradation behavior?

options.A: Silently proceed as if the escalation path exists and attempt to answer without human input.
options.B: Terminate the session immediately with an error message.
options.C: Inform the user that the normal escalation path is unavailable, offer an alternative (e.g., callback, email), and provide the best self-service response possible while being transparent about limitations.
options.D: Retry the escalation path every 5 seconds until it becomes available, blocking the user in the meantime.

questions.explanation: Graceful degradation means maintaining the best possible service level when a primary escalation path is unavailable. This involves: being transparent with the user about the limitation, offering alternative resolution paths, and providing the best self-service response with clear caveats about what the agent cannot guarantee. This preserves trust and keeps the user informed.

whyWrongMap.A: Silently proceeding is a form of deception and can lead to harmful outcomes if the agent provides an answer that required human validation.
whyWrongMap.B: Immediately terminating is the worst possible degradation; it abandons the user entirely when partial service is still possible.
whyWrongMap.D: Blocking the user in a retry loop creates a poor experience and offers no value; the user needs to be informed and offered alternatives, not held waiting indefinitely.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Graceful degradation when escalation is not available
Anthropic Claude documentation: Fallback behavior and service continuity

questions.scenario: A user asks an agent: 'Can you help me deal with my neighbor?' The agent must decide whether to ask for clarification or make an assumption and proceed.
questions.text: What principle should guide this decision?

options.A: Always assume the most benign interpretation and proceed to maximize conversational flow.
options.B: Always ask for clarification when any ambiguity exists, regardless of stakes.
options.C: Weigh the cost of a wrong assumption against the cost of asking; ask for clarification when the risk of assuming incorrectly is high relative to the friction of the question.
options.D: Escalate to a human agent because neighbor disputes may involve safety concerns.

questions.explanation: The decision to clarify vs. assume should be a risk-weighted judgment. 'Help me deal with my neighbor' could mean a noise complaint, a legal boundary dispute, or interpersonal conflict advice — the consequences of assuming incorrectly differ significantly. The agent should ask a single focused clarifying question when the space of possible interpretations is wide and the cost of a wrong assumption is non-trivial.

whyWrongMap.A: Always assuming the most benign interpretation is a heuristic, not a principle; it ignores cases where benign assumptions lead to wrong or unhelpful responses.
whyWrongMap.B: Asking for clarification on every ambiguity creates excessive friction; minor ambiguities with low consequence should often be assumed and confirmed inline.
whyWrongMap.D: Immediate escalation over a vague statement is premature; there is no explicit safety signal yet — a clarifying question is the appropriate first step.

references: Task Statement 5.2: When to ask for clarification vs when to assume
Anthropic Claude documentation: Ambiguity resolution strategies

questions.scenario: An architect is designing a multi-agent customer service system. They need to decide when the primary agent should route to a supervisor agent versus routing directly to human review.
questions.text: Which criterion best distinguishes when to route to a supervisor agent versus human review?

options.A: Supervisor agents handle all issues; human review is only for abusive language.
options.B: Route to supervisor agent when the issue requires broader automated knowledge or policy arbitration; route to human review when the issue requires human judgment, empathy, or accountability.
options.C: Always route to human review first; supervisor agents are only a fallback if humans are unavailable.
options.D: Route to supervisor agent for technical issues and human review for billing issues.

questions.explanation: The routing decision should be based on the nature of the required resolution. Supervisor agents are appropriate when a broader automated knowledge base, policy lookup, or cross-domain reasoning can resolve the issue. Human review is appropriate when the resolution requires genuine human judgment, accountability (e.g., authorizing a large refund), or emotional intelligence. Categorizing by issue type (technical vs. billing) is a poor proxy for this underlying distinction.

whyWrongMap.A: This severely mischaracterizes human review scope; humans are involved for far more than abusive language cases.
whyWrongMap.C: This inverts the proper escalation order; supervisor agents are typically a cheaper and faster first escalation step before consuming human capacity.
whyWrongMap.D: Issue type (technical vs. billing) is a weak proxy; many billing issues can be resolved by a supervisor agent, and some technical issues require human judgment.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Escalation routing — human review, supervisor agent, fallback behavior
Anthropic Claude documentation: Designing escalation routing logic

questions.scenario: A financial services agent is designed with the following escalation policy: escalate if confidence < 0.75, if conflicting data is detected, or if the transaction value exceeds $10,000. A customer asks about rolling over a $12,000 IRA account. The agent has high confidence (0.89) and no conflicting data, but the transaction value trigger fires. The customer has a documented preference for self-service and has previously completed similar rollovers autonomously.
questions.text: How should the architect resolve the tension between the transaction value escalation trigger and the customer's documented self-service preference?

options.A: The transaction value trigger is absolute and must override any customer preference; escalate unconditionally.
options.B: Customer preferences always override system triggers; proceed with self-service.
options.C: The architect should design the system so that hard regulatory or risk-based triggers (like transaction value) are not overridable by preference, but the escalation UX is personalized — e.g., offering a lightweight confirmation step rather than a full hand-off, and documenting the customer's consent.
options.D: Remove the transaction value trigger entirely since it conflicts with personalization goals.

questions.explanation: This is a nuanced architectural tension. Hard triggers based on regulatory, compliance, or risk thresholds (such as transaction value limits) generally cannot be bypassed by customer preferences — they exist to protect both the customer and the institution. However, personalization still applies to how the escalation is implemented: instead of a full hand-off to a human, the system might provide a streamlined confirmation flow with explicit consent capture, satisfying both the compliance requirement and the customer's preference for minimal friction. The key is separating the non-negotiable trigger from the negotiable escalation experience.

whyWrongMap.A: While the trigger should fire, 'unconditional escalation to full human review' ignores the valid architectural option of a lightweight, compliant self-service confirmation that satisfies both concerns.
whyWrongMap.B: Allowing customer preferences to override compliance-based risk triggers is a regulatory and liability failure; preferences personalize the experience but cannot bypass mandatory controls.
whyWrongMap.D: Removing a risk-based trigger to satisfy personalization goals is architecturally irresponsible and likely non-compliant with financial services regulations.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Customer preference tracking for personalized escalation
Task Statement 5.2: Escalation triggers and routing
Anthropic Claude documentation: Compliance constraints in agent design

questions.scenario: An architect is reviewing an agent system where operators have reported 'escalation storms' — brief periods where a large percentage of conversations simultaneously trigger escalation, overwhelming the human review queue. Analysis shows the triggers are technically firing correctly: confidence drops system-wide when the underlying knowledge base is stale.
questions.text: Which architectural pattern best addresses escalation storms caused by systemic knowledge base staleness?

options.A: Raise the confidence threshold so fewer conversations trigger escalation.
options.B: Implement circuit-breaker logic: detect systemic escalation rate spikes, switch to a degraded-mode response with transparent caveats, alert operations to the staleness issue, and resume normal escalation once the knowledge base is refreshed.
options.C: Add sentiment analysis as a secondary filter to reduce escalation volume.
options.D: Route all escalations to a supervisor agent instead of human review to absorb the volume.

questions.explanation: Escalation storms caused by systemic issues (like a stale knowledge base) require a circuit-breaker pattern, not threshold adjustment. Raising the threshold would suppress legitimate escalations even after the underlying issue is resolved. The circuit-breaker detects the abnormal escalation rate, recognizes it as systemic rather than case-by-case, switches to graceful degradation mode (transparent about limitations), and triggers an operational alert to fix the root cause. This is a resilience pattern that preserves both system stability and user trust.

whyWrongMap.A: Raising the threshold treats a symptom (escalation volume) by suppressing signals; it does not address the root cause and permanently reduces escalation sensitivity for future legitimate cases.
whyWrongMap.C: Adding sentiment analysis as a filter introduces the exact unreliability problem described in the task statement; it would incorrectly block valid escalations based on tone rather than actual need.
whyWrongMap.D: Routing all escalations to a supervisor agent only shifts the bottleneck; if the knowledge base is stale, the supervisor agent has the same information gap, and the volume issue remains.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Graceful degradation when escalation is not available
Task Statement 5.2: Escalation triggers and routing
Anthropic Claude documentation: Resilience patterns in multi-agent systems

questions.scenario: A user interacting with a healthcare agent sends the following message: 'I've been taking twice the prescribed dose because the normal dose doesn't seem to work. I just need to know if it's safe to keep doing this.' The agent has relevant pharmaceutical data but the situation involves potential patient harm.
questions.text: Which combination of escalation triggers is active, and what is the correct multi-trigger escalation handling?

options.A: Only the safety concern trigger is active; route directly to human review and provide no information.
options.B: Safety concern and ambiguous input triggers are both active; ask for clarification on which medication before deciding.
options.C: Safety concern trigger is active; the agent should immediately provide the pharmaceutical data since the user explicitly asked for it, then note the trigger.
options.D: Safety concern trigger is active, potentially alongside a confidence trigger; provide harm-reduction information to avoid immediate risk, clearly state the agent's limitations, explicitly recommend consulting a healthcare provider, and route to human review — do not withhold all information in a way that leaves the user without any guidance.

questions.explanation: This scenario activates the safety concern trigger. However, the correct response is nuanced: completely withholding information when a user faces potential immediate harm can itself cause harm (the user may continue the dangerous behavior with no guidance). The agent should provide harm-reduction information appropriate to the safety context, be transparent about its limitations, strongly recommend professional consultation, and simultaneously escalate to human review. This balances the safety trigger against the ethical obligation not to abandon a user in a potentially dangerous situation.

whyWrongMap.A: Providing no information at all when a patient may be harming themselves is an overly rigid interpretation of 'safety concern'; it can worsen outcomes by leaving the user without any guidance while waiting for human review.
whyWrongMap.B: The lack of medication name is not the primary concern here; the safety signal is explicit and immediate action is required regardless of which medication is involved.
whyWrongMap.C: Simply providing pharmaceutical data without safety framing, human escalation, or professional referral fails to address the severity of the safety concern appropriately.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Escalation triggers — safety concerns
Task Statement 5.2: Escalation routing — human review
Anthropic Claude documentation: Safety-critical escalation in healthcare contexts

questions.scenario: An architect is designing an escalation system for a global customer service platform. The platform must operate 24/7 across time zones where human agents are not always available. The system must handle: (1) confidence-based escalation, (2) conflicting information, (3) safety concerns, and (4) customer preference for human contact — all while ensuring graceful degradation during low-staffing windows.
questions.text: Which architectural design most comprehensively addresses all four requirements while maintaining quality of service during low-staffing periods?

options.A: Use a single escalation queue for all trigger types; apply a time-based filter to suppress non-safety escalations during off-hours.
options.B: Design a tiered escalation architecture: (1) supervisor agent as first escalation tier for confidence and conflicting information triggers; (2) asynchronous human review queue with SLA-based callbacks for preference-driven escalations during off-hours; (3) immediate synchronous human routing for safety escalations regardless of staffing; (4) graceful degradation messaging for all tiers when capacity is exceeded, with transparent user communication.
options.C: Rely on supervisor agents for all escalation types to eliminate human dependency entirely.
options.D: Add more human agents across all time zones to eliminate the staffing gap.

questions.explanation: A tiered escalation architecture is the correct design because different trigger types have fundamentally different urgency and resolution profiles. Confidence and conflicting-information triggers can often be resolved by a supervisor agent (first tier) without human involvement. Preference-based human contact requests can be handled asynchronously with a callback commitment, honoring the preference without requiring instantaneous human availability. Safety triggers must always route synchronously to humans regardless of staffing — this is non-negotiable. Graceful degradation messaging ensures users are informed at every tier when capacity is limited. This design is both operationally efficient and ethically sound.

whyWrongMap.A: Suppressing non-safety escalations based purely on time of day ignores the different urgency profiles of trigger types; a conflicting information issue at 3am still needs a defined resolution path, not suppression.
whyWrongMap.C: Supervisor agents cannot replace human judgment for all escalation types; preference-driven requests explicitly require human contact, and safety escalations require human accountability — eliminating human dependency entirely is architecturally and ethically wrong.
whyWrongMap.D: Adding human agents across all time zones addresses the staffing gap but is not an architectural solution; it does not address the tiering, routing logic, or graceful degradation requirements, and is economically unscalable.

references: Task Statement 5.2: Escalation routing — human review, supervisor agent, fallback behavior
Task Statement 5.2: Graceful degradation when escalation is not available
Task Statement 5.2: Customer preference tracking for personalized escalation
Anthropic Claude documentation: Designing resilient multi-tier escalation systems

questions.scenario: An agent in a multi-agent pipeline attempts to fetch customer records from a database. The database returns an HTTP 403 Forbidden response. A downstream summarization agent is waiting for the results.
questions.text: How should the fetching agent classify and communicate this outcome in its error payload?

options.A: Return an empty array so the downstream agent can handle missing data uniformly
options.B: Return a structured error payload with type 'ACCESS_DENIED', retryable: false, and an empty partialResults field
options.C: Retry the request three times before returning any error to the downstream agent
options.D: Return a structured error payload with type 'EMPTY_RESULT', retryable: true, since no data was returned

questions.explanation: A 403 Forbidden is an access failure, not an empty result. It must be clearly classified as 'ACCESS_DENIED' with retryable: false — retrying will not resolve a permission denial. Returning an empty array would mask the failure, causing the downstream agent to incorrectly treat a permission error as a valid query with no matches. A structured error payload with the correct type, retryable flag, and empty partial results preserves error context across the agent boundary.

whyWrongMap.A: Returning an empty array conflates two fundamentally different outcomes: a valid query that found no matches, and an access failure that prevented any query from completing. Downstream agents cannot distinguish between these cases and may make incorrect decisions based on the apparent 'empty' result.
whyWrongMap.C: Retrying after a 403 Forbidden is wasteful and incorrect. Access failures are non-retryable by nature — the permission will not change between retries. Delaying the error propagation adds latency without any chance of recovery.
whyWrongMap.D: Classifying a 403 as 'EMPTY_RESULT' with retryable: true is a category error. EMPTY_RESULT indicates a valid query returned no matches. A 403 is an access failure that must be distinguished from empty results, and it is not retryable.

questions.scenario: A search agent queries three data sources in parallel. Two sources return results successfully. The third source times out after the configured deadline. The orchestrator needs a response immediately.
questions.text: What is the correct behavior for the search agent when one of three parallel data sources times out?

options.A: Wait indefinitely for the timed-out source before returning anything to the orchestrator
options.B: Discard all results and return a single TIMEOUT error to signal complete failure
options.C: Return the results from the two successful sources along with metadata indicating which source timed out and that its results are missing
options.D: Return only the results from the two successful sources without any indication that a third source was queried

questions.explanation: Partial results with metadata is the correct pattern. The agent has valid, useful data from two sources and should return it immediately rather than blocking or discarding it. The metadata must include which source timed out and that its contribution is absent, so the orchestrator and downstream agents can make informed decisions about the completeness of the data they received.

whyWrongMap.A: Waiting indefinitely for a timed-out source defeats the purpose of timeout handling. It blocks the entire pipeline and prevents any results from flowing downstream, turning a partial failure into a total stall.
whyWrongMap.B: Discarding all results because one source timed out is disproportionate and wasteful. Two-thirds of the data was retrieved successfully. The partial results pattern exists precisely to prevent this unnecessary total loss.
whyWrongMap.D: Returning partial results silently — without metadata about the missing source — is a form of silent failure. Downstream agents would treat the partial data as complete, potentially making incorrect decisions based on an incomplete picture.

questions.scenario: A validation agent in a pipeline receives a structured error payload from an upstream data-fetching agent. The payload includes: type: 'NETWORK_ERROR', message: 'Connection refused on port 5432', retryable: true, partialResults: [].
questions.text: What should the validation agent do with the retryable: true flag in this error payload?

options.A: Ignore the flag and immediately propagate the error downstream without retrying
options.B: Use the flag to decide whether to trigger a retry of the upstream operation or propagate the error, based on its retry budget
options.C: Treat retryable: true as confirmation that the operation will eventually succeed and wait indefinitely
options.D: Replace retryable: true with retryable: false before passing the error downstream to prevent retry loops

questions.explanation: The retryable flag is informational — it signals that the error condition could resolve on a subsequent attempt (e.g., a transient network issue). The receiving agent should consult its retry budget (e.g., maximum attempt count, backoff policy) to decide whether to retry the upstream call or propagate the error further. Retryable: true does not guarantee success, only that a retry is worth attempting.

whyWrongMap.A: Ignoring the retryable flag discards valuable error context. A transient network error that resolves on retry is a common and recoverable situation; immediately propagating it as a permanent failure is unnecessarily pessimistic.
whyWrongMap.C: Retryable: true means a retry is worth attempting, not that success is guaranteed or that waiting indefinitely is appropriate. Waiting without a bound transforms a recoverable error into a pipeline stall.
whyWrongMap.D: Altering the retryable flag before passing an error downstream corrupts the error context. Downstream agents and operators rely on accurate error metadata to make decisions; silently changing it introduces hidden inconsistencies.

questions.scenario: An analyst agent queries a database for all transactions in a date range. The query executes successfully, but the database contains zero records matching the criteria. The agent must return a result to an orchestrating agent.
questions.text: How should the analyst agent correctly represent this outcome?

options.A: Return a structured error payload with type: 'NO_DATA_FOUND' and retryable: false
options.B: Return an error payload indicating the query failed to retrieve any results
options.C: Return a successful response with an empty results array and a message indicating no records matched the criteria
options.D: Return a successful response with null to indicate no data was available

questions.explanation: An empty result set from a successful query is not an error — it is a valid outcome. The correct representation is a success response with an empty results array and a clear message that no records matched the criteria. This preserves the critical distinction between access failures (which prevent query execution) and valid queries that return no data.

whyWrongMap.A: Returning an error for a successful query with no matches conflates two different concepts. A query that executed successfully but found no records is not a failure; treating it as one forces downstream agents to handle a non-error case through error-handling paths.
whyWrongMap.B: The query did not fail — it succeeded. Wrapping an empty result in an error payload is semantically incorrect and would cause downstream agents to invoke error-handling logic for a normal, expected outcome.
whyWrongMap.D: Returning null rather than an empty array is ambiguous. It does not clearly convey whether the query succeeded, whether data access failed, or whether null means 'no results'. An explicit empty array with a descriptive message is unambiguous.

questions.scenario: A five-agent pipeline processes loan applications: ingestion → validation → credit-check → decision → notification. The credit-check agent fails with a transient timeout connecting to an external bureau. The decision agent is waiting for the credit score.
questions.text: Which error propagation strategy best prevents a cascading failure across the remaining pipeline stages?

options.A: The credit-check agent retries immediately in a tight loop until the bureau responds, blocking the decision agent
options.B: The credit-check agent returns a structured error payload with type: 'TIMEOUT', retryable: true, and partialResults containing any cached credit data; the orchestrator decides whether to retry or route to a fallback path
options.C: The credit-check agent propagates a generic error message to the decision agent, which propagates it to the notification agent, which alerts the user
options.D: The credit-check agent silently returns a neutral credit score of 0 to allow the pipeline to continue processing

questions.explanation: Returning a structured error payload with complete metadata — type, retryable flag, and any cached partial results — gives the orchestrator the information it needs to make an intelligent recovery decision (retry with backoff, route to a fallback, or halt). This prevents cascading failure by stopping error propagation at the point of failure and centralizing recovery decisions rather than forcing every downstream agent to handle the error independently.

whyWrongMap.A: Retrying in a tight loop without backoff or a retry budget can exhaust resources and increase load on an already-struggling external service. Blocking the decision agent while retrying indefinitely converts a transient fault into a pipeline stall.
whyWrongMap.C: Propagating a generic error without structured metadata through each agent strips out context at every boundary. By the time the notification agent receives it, the original error type, retryability, and any partial data are lost, making automated recovery impossible.
whyWrongMap.D: Silently substituting a neutral score is a form of data fabrication. It allows the pipeline to continue but produces decisions based on incorrect information. This is one of the most dangerous patterns in multi-agent systems — it hides failures while corrupting outputs.

questions.scenario: An orchestrator agent delegates subtasks to three worker agents: A (data fetch), B (enrichment), and C (formatting). Agent A completes successfully. Agent B fails with a non-retryable permission error. Agent C has not yet started.
questions.text: How should the orchestrator handle Agent B's non-retryable permission error to avoid unnecessary cascading work?

options.A: Allow Agent C to start and run to completion, then combine its output with Agent A's output, omitting Agent B's contribution
options.B: Cancel Agent C before it starts, return Agent A's partial results to the caller with metadata indicating Agent B failed with a permission error and Agent C was cancelled
options.C: Restart all three agents from the beginning to ensure a consistent result
options.D: Return only Agent A's results without any indication that Agents B and C did not complete

questions.explanation: When a non-retryable failure occurs in a dependency, the orchestrator should cancel downstream work that depends on that failure — Agent C requires Agent B's enrichment output to be meaningful. Starting Agent C anyway wastes resources and produces an incomplete result without the caller knowing why. The correct pattern is to cancel C, preserve A's partial results, and return a structured response that describes exactly what succeeded, what failed (with error type and non-retryable flag), and what was cancelled.

whyWrongMap.A: Running Agent C when it depends on Agent B's output produces either an error in C or a result that is missing enrichment data. This wastes compute and delivers output of unknown quality. The caller receives no signal that the result is incomplete.
whyWrongMap.C: Restarting all agents when only Agent B has a non-retryable permission error is wasteful. Agent A succeeded and need not repeat its work. A permission error will not resolve on retry, so restarting Agent B is pointless.
whyWrongMap.D: Returning Agent A's results silently without metadata about the downstream failures is a form of silent partial failure. The caller cannot distinguish between a complete result and a partial one, leading to decisions made on incomplete data.

questions.scenario: An agent crosses three agent boundaries in a chain: Fetcher → Transformer → Aggregator. The Fetcher encounters a network error. It logs the error locally and then re-throws a new generic RuntimeException to the Transformer, which does the same to the Aggregator.
questions.text: What error propagation anti-pattern is being demonstrated, and what is the correct alternative?

options.A: Exception tunneling — the correct alternative is to re-throw the original exception unchanged at each boundary
options.B: Error context loss — the correct alternative is to propagate the original structured error payload intact across boundaries, or to wrap it in a new payload that explicitly includes the original as a cause
options.C: Over-logging — the correct alternative is to log the error only once at the final boundary
options.D: Error swallowing — the correct alternative is to catch and discard errors at each boundary to prevent the chain from breaking

questions.explanation: Catching a specific structured error and re-throwing a generic RuntimeException destroys the error context — the original error type, retryable flag, and partial results are all lost. Each boundary strips away more information. The correct pattern is to propagate the original structured error payload unchanged, or to wrap it in an envelope that explicitly carries the original payload as the 'cause', so that any agent in the chain (and the final caller) has full diagnostic information.

whyWrongMap.A: Exception tunneling describes passing errors through layers that don't understand them — not the same as converting structured errors to generic ones. Re-throwing the original exception unchanged would actually be a better pattern than described in the scenario, but the problem is that a new generic exception is created at each step, not that it's tunneled.
whyWrongMap.C: Logging is a separate concern from error propagation. The problem here is not the logging strategy but the conversion of structured error information into a generic exception that discards all context. Logging at a single boundary would not solve the loss of error type and retryability metadata.
whyWrongMap.D: Error swallowing means catching errors and not propagating them at all. The scenario explicitly states errors are being re-thrown, so they are not being swallowed. Swallowing errors would be a different (and also incorrect) anti-pattern.

questions.scenario: A report generation pipeline has an orchestrator that calls five specialist agents sequentially. The orchestrator enforces a total pipeline timeout of 30 seconds. Each agent has a 10-second individual timeout. After 25 seconds, two agents have completed and the third is still running.
questions.text: When the third agent exceeds the remaining 5-second pipeline budget, what should the orchestrator do?

options.A: Wait for the third agent to finish regardless of the pipeline timeout, since its result may be needed
options.B: Cancel the third agent, skip the remaining two agents, and return the partial results from the two completed agents with metadata indicating which agents timed out and were skipped
options.C: Extend the pipeline timeout to 60 seconds to allow all agents to complete
options.D: Cancel all five agents and return a total failure, since the pipeline did not complete within budget

questions.explanation: When the pipeline-level timeout budget is exhausted, the orchestrator must cancel the running agent and skip unstarted work. The correct response is to return the partial results already accumulated with metadata that identifies: which agents completed, which agent timed out mid-execution, and which agents were never started. This preserves the value of completed work, respects the caller's time contract, and gives the caller enough information to decide on recovery.

whyWrongMap.A: Waiting for the third agent after the pipeline deadline has passed violates the caller's timeout contract. The orchestrator made a commitment to respond within 30 seconds; exceeding that unilaterally undermines the reliability contract of the pipeline.
whyWrongMap.C: Dynamically extending the timeout at the point of expiration defeats the purpose of having a timeout. Timeouts exist to provide callers with a reliable upper bound on latency. An orchestrator that extends its own timeout dynamically offers no meaningful latency guarantee.
whyWrongMap.D: Discarding the two successfully completed agents' results when two-thirds of the work succeeded is unnecessarily destructive. Partial results with clear metadata are more useful than a total failure that forces the entire pipeline to restart from scratch.

questions.scenario: An agent system processes medical records. A patient-lookup agent successfully retrieves 847 out of 850 records before hitting a memory limit. The remaining 3 records could not be fetched. The agent must respond to the requesting aggregation agent.
questions.text: Which response structure correctly implements the partial results pattern for this scenario?

options.A: { success: true, results: [847 records] } — return the records silently, since 847/850 is nearly complete
options.B: { success: false, error: { type: 'MEMORY_LIMIT', message: '...', retryable: false } } — return an error since the operation did not fully complete
options.C: { success: true, results: [847 records], metadata: { total_requested: 850, total_returned: 847, missing_ids: [id1, id2, id3], reason: 'MEMORY_LIMIT' } }
options.D: { success: true, results: [850 records] } — fabricate placeholder records for the 3 missing entries to maintain count consistency

questions.explanation: The partial results pattern requires returning both the available data and metadata that precisely describes what is missing and why. The response should be marked as successful (the agent did return valid data) but include: total requested, total returned, the specific IDs that are missing, and the reason for incompleteness. This gives the aggregation agent the information it needs to decide whether to proceed with partial data, request the missing records separately, or escalate.

whyWrongMap.A: Returning 847 records without any indication that 3 are missing is a silent partial failure. The aggregation agent would treat the result as complete, potentially producing analyses based on an incomplete patient population — a critical error in a medical context.
whyWrongMap.B: Marking the response as a pure failure discards 847 valid records. The agent did successfully retrieve the vast majority of data; calling it a total failure loses that value and forces a complete retry. The partial results pattern exists to avoid this waste.
whyWrongMap.D: Fabricating placeholder records to maintain count consistency is data falsification. In a medical context this is particularly dangerous — downstream agents would process artificial records as if they were real patient data, producing incorrect analyses or decisions.

questions.scenario: An orchestrator manages a fan-out to 10 worker agents. Workers 1–7 complete successfully. Worker 8 fails with a transient NETWORK_ERROR (retryable: true). Worker 9 fails with a permanent ACCESS_DENIED (retryable: false). Worker 10 is still running when the pipeline timeout fires. The orchestrator must respond to its caller immediately with whatever it has.
questions.text: Which response structure correctly encodes all three failure modalities while preserving the partial results?

options.A: Return only the 7 successful results; errors and timeouts are internal concerns not relevant to the caller
options.B: Return a single error payload with the first error encountered (NETWORK_ERROR), since only a single error can be communicated at a time
options.C: { results: [7 results], metadata: { completed: 7, failed: [{ worker: 8, error: { type: 'NETWORK_ERROR', retryable: true } }, { worker: 9, error: { type: 'ACCESS_DENIED', retryable: false } }], timed_out: [10], total: 10 } }
options.D: { results: [7 results], metadata: { completed: 7, errors: 2, timed_out: 1 } } — with error counts only, since detailed error types are a security concern

questions.explanation: A multi-failure scenario requires a response that carries all three failure modalities distinctly: the two failed workers with their individual structured error payloads (preserving type and retryable flags so the caller can decide to retry Worker 8 but not Worker 9), and the timed-out worker as a separate category. The caller needs this granularity to make informed recovery decisions — retrying only the retryable failure, escalating the access denial, and deciding whether to re-request Worker 10's output. Aggregating failures into a single error or counts discards the information needed for precise recovery.

whyWrongMap.A: Hiding error and timeout information from the caller makes intelligent recovery impossible. The caller cannot retry Worker 8's retryable error, cannot escalate Worker 9's access denial, and cannot decide whether to re-request Worker 10's output if it has no knowledge these failures occurred.
whyWrongMap.B: Returning only the first error encountered discards information about Workers 9 and 10 entirely. In a multi-agent fan-out, multiple independent failures can occur simultaneously; a single error payload cannot represent this state accurately and prevents differentiated recovery strategies.
whyWrongMap.D: Providing only error counts without error types or retryability flags is insufficient for recovery. The caller cannot distinguish a transient network error (worth retrying) from a permanent access denial (not worth retrying) from a count of '2 errors'. The claim that error types are a security concern is not a valid reason to strip out actionable metadata from internal inter-agent communication.

questions.scenario: A six-stage pipeline: Ingest → Parse → Enrich → Score → Decide → Notify. Enrich calls three external APIs in parallel with individual 5-second timeouts. Two APIs respond; one times out. Enrich propagates a partial result to Score. Score applies its logic on the available data and produces a score with a confidence interval. Decide receives the scored result.
questions.text: What must the Decide agent verify before acting on the scored result to prevent decisions based on silently degraded data?

options.A: Verify that the score value is within the expected numeric range, since out-of-range values indicate incomplete enrichment
options.B: Check the metadata chain: confirm Score's response includes a completeness flag or partial-results indicator inherited from Enrich's timeout, and apply a decision policy appropriate for degraded-confidence scores
options.C: Re-run the Enrich stage from scratch to guarantee a fully enriched input before scoring
options.D: Accept the score at face value; individual agent quality control is the responsibility of each agent, not of downstream consumers

questions.explanation: Error context must be preserved across agent boundaries. When Enrich produces a partial result due to a timeout, that incompleteness marker must travel with the data through Score and be visible to Decide. The Decide agent must inspect the metadata chain — confirming that Score flagged a degraded-confidence result — and apply an appropriate decision policy (e.g., route to manual review, widen confidence thresholds, or decline to automate). Acting on a degraded score as if it were a complete score is a cascading failure pattern where a data quality problem silently drives incorrect downstream decisions.

whyWrongMap.A: A score produced from two-thirds of the enrichment data may still fall within the expected numeric range — partial data does not necessarily produce an out-of-range value. Numeric range validation cannot detect data completeness issues. Only explicit partial-result metadata in the payload can reliably signal this condition.
whyWrongMap.C: Re-running the entire Enrich stage from Decide introduces an incorrect responsibility boundary — Decide should not be orchestrating upstream data collection. If re-enrichment is the desired recovery strategy, it should be triggered by the orchestrator or Score, not by a downstream decision agent. This also violates the principle of forward-only data flow in a pipeline.
whyWrongMap.D: Assuming each upstream agent guarantees complete quality and accepting results at face value is the root cause of cascading failures. In a system that supports partial results by design, downstream agents must inspect completeness metadata rather than assuming completeness — otherwise degraded data silently propagates to decisions.

questions.scenario: An architect is designing a new multi-agent system for financial trade processing. The system must distinguish between: (1) a trade lookup that returns no trades for a valid account (normal empty day), (2) a permission failure preventing any trades from being read, (3) a network timeout that may have only partially read the trade log, and (4) a corrupted response from the trade database. All four outcomes must be handled differently by downstream risk and compliance agents.
questions.text: Which error taxonomy and payload design correctly supports all four distinct outcomes?

options.A: Use HTTP status codes alone: 200 for empty results, 403 for permission, 408 for timeout, 500 for corruption — downstream agents inspect the status code
options.B: Use a two-field payload: { ok: boolean, data: any } — ok: true with empty data for empty results, ok: false with an error string for all failure cases
options.C: Use a structured payload with: { status: 'SUCCESS'|'PARTIAL'|'ERROR', data: [...], metadata: { completeness: 'FULL'|'PARTIAL'|'NONE', reason?: string }, error?: { type: 'ACCESS_DENIED'|'NETWORK_TIMEOUT'|'DATA_CORRUPTION'|null, retryable: boolean, partialResults: [...] } } — each outcome maps to a unique combination of fields
options.D: Use a single error type 'DATA_UNAVAILABLE' with a free-text message field for all four cases, letting downstream agents parse the message to determine the actual condition
