questions.explanation: A structured payload with orthogonal fields for status, completeness metadata, and a typed error object is the only design that can unambiguously represent all four outcomes: (1) SUCCESS + FULL + no error for a valid empty day; (2) ERROR + NONE + ACCESS_DENIED (retryable: false) for a permission failure; (3) PARTIAL + PARTIAL + NETWORK_TIMEOUT (retryable: true) + whatever partial data was received for a mid-read timeout; (4) ERROR + NONE + DATA_CORRUPTION (retryable: false) for a corrupted response. Each outcome is machine-parseable with no ambiguity, allowing downstream risk and compliance agents to take differentiated, automated actions.

whyWrongMap.A: HTTP status codes conflate transport-layer semantics with business logic. A 403 might mean the agent lacks credentials, or that the account itself is restricted — these require different downstream actions. HTTP codes also cannot represent partial reads or distinguish data corruption from other 500-class errors. They are insufficient for the semantic precision required by financial compliance agents.
whyWrongMap.B: A two-field ok/data payload collapses all three failure modes (access denied, timeout, corruption) into a single 'ok: false' state with only a string message. Downstream agents cannot take differentiated automated actions (retry only timeouts, escalate corruptions, alert on access denials) based on a boolean and a free-text string.
whyWrongMap.D: Using a single error type with a free-text message for all four cases forces downstream agents to perform brittle string parsing to determine the actual error condition. Any change to the message format breaks downstream logic. This anti-pattern makes automated recovery unreliable and violates the principle of structured, machine-readable error payloads.

questions.scenario: A production multi-agent system begins experiencing intermittent failures. Investigation reveals: Agent A times out calling Agent B. Agent B is timing out because it is waiting on Agent C. Agent C is overwhelmed because Agent A has been retrying its calls to Agent B exponentially, flooding Agent C through B. The system is in a feedback loop.
questions.text: Which combination of design mechanisms would have prevented this cascading failure pattern?

options.A: Increase all agent timeouts to 60 seconds so agents have more time to recover before failing
options.B: Add a circuit breaker on Agent B's calls to Agent C that opens after a threshold of failures, combined with exponential backoff with jitter on Agent A's retries to Agent B, and a retry budget that caps total retry attempts
options.C: Remove all retries from Agent A so it fails fast on the first timeout, preventing the retry flood
options.D: Add request queuing in Agent B so all of Agent A's retry requests are buffered rather than immediately forwarded to Agent C

questions.explanation: The failure pattern described is a retry storm amplified through a dependency chain — a classic cascading failure. Three mechanisms together prevent it: (1) A circuit breaker on B→C opens when C is overwhelmed, stopping B from forwarding requests to an already-struggling dependency and giving C time to recover. (2) Exponential backoff with jitter on A's retries reduces the burst rate and spreads retry load over time. (3) A retry budget caps the total number of attempts, preventing A from retrying indefinitely. These three mechanisms together break the feedback loop: jitter spreads load, backoff reduces rate, the circuit breaker stops amplification through the dependency chain, and the budget ensures eventual termination.

whyWrongMap.A: Increasing timeouts makes the problem worse. Longer timeouts mean Agent A waits longer before retrying, but when it does retry, it accumulates more concurrent in-flight requests. A 60-second timeout with exponential retries would create a much larger burst when retries finally trigger, amplifying the flood to Agent C.
whyWrongMap.C: Removing all retries makes Agent A fragile against transient failures that would have recovered quickly. The problem is not that retries exist but that they are uncontrolled — no backoff, no jitter, no budget. The correct fix is bounded, backoff-regulated retries combined with circuit breaking, not the elimination of retries entirely.
whyWrongMap.D: Buffering in Agent B defers the problem rather than solving it. A queue absorbs short bursts but does not limit the total load forwarded to Agent C over time. An unbounded queue would eventually forward all of Agent A's retry flood to Agent C, just slightly delayed. Queue depth limits would still result in request drops, and the underlying feedback loop (A retrying aggressively based on B's delays) is not addressed.

questions.scenario: You are beginning a fresh exploration session in a large monorepo with hundreds of files. You need to understand the overall structure before diving into specific modules.
questions.text: What is the recommended first step when starting incremental exploration of a large codebase?

options.A: Read every file in the repository from top to bottom
options.B: Use Glob to identify file patterns and structure before reading specific files
options.C: Run Grep searches across all files simultaneously
options.D: Immediately delegate the entire exploration to a subagent

questions.explanation: The recommended incremental understanding workflow is Glob → Read → Grep. Glob provides a broad structural overview with minimal context cost, allowing you to identify relevant files before committing to reading their full contents. This prevents context pollution from reading irrelevant files.

whyWrongMap.A: Reading every file consumes enormous context and pollutes the context window with irrelevant content, leading to premature context exhaustion.
whyWrongMap.C: Running Grep across all files without first understanding the structure can yield overwhelming results and consumes context on noise rather than signal.
whyWrongMap.D: Delegating before you understand the scope may result in poorly scoped subagent instructions and redundant exploration work.

references: Incremental understanding: Glob → Read → Grep workflow

questions.scenario: A Claude agent has been exploring a large codebase for 45 minutes and has accumulated extensive context about authentication flows, database schemas, and API routes. The user now asks about a completely unrelated billing module.
questions.text: What is the primary purpose of writing findings to scratchpad files during a long exploration session?

options.A: To share findings with other team members
options.B: To satisfy documentation requirements
options.C: To preserve discovered knowledge so it survives context resets or compaction
options.D: To create a permanent audit log of all exploration activity

questions.explanation: Scratchpad files serve as persistent external memory. When context grows large and must be compacted or reset, findings written to files remain intact and can be re-read in a future session. This is the core strategy for surviving context degradation over long exploration sessions.

whyWrongMap.A: While files can be shared, the primary architectural reason for scratchpad files in this context is agent memory persistence across context boundaries, not team collaboration.
whyWrongMap.B: Documentation requirements are a secondary concern; the core motivation is surviving context resets, not compliance.
whyWrongMap.D: Audit logging is a distinct concern with different requirements; scratchpad files are specifically for agent working memory, not permanent records.

references: Scratchpad files: write findings to files so they survive context resets

questions.scenario: A developer notices that after reading several large configuration files and auto-generated type definition files, the Claude agent's responses have become less precise and it is struggling to remember earlier findings.
questions.text: What phenomenon best describes what is happening to the agent's performance?

options.A: Model hallucination caused by incorrect training data
options.B: Context degradation caused by large file reads polluting the context window
options.C: Network latency from too many file read operations
options.D: Rate limiting from the underlying API provider

questions.explanation: Context degradation occurs when large, low-signal content (like auto-generated files or verbose configs) is read into the context window, displacing earlier high-value findings and reducing the model's effective working memory. Avoiding context pollution from large file reads is a core context management principle.

whyWrongMap.A: Hallucination refers to generating false information; the described symptom is degraded recall of real findings, which is a context window capacity issue.
whyWrongMap.C: File read operations are local and have negligible latency; this is not a network problem.
whyWrongMap.D: Rate limiting would cause request failures or delays, not degraded precision in responses.

references: Avoiding context pollution from large file reads

questions.scenario: An agent has built up a large context window after hours of exploration and needs to continue working but is approaching the context limit.
questions.text: What does the /compact command primarily accomplish in a long exploration session?

options.A: Compresses source code files to reduce disk space
options.B: Summarizes the current conversation and frees context window space for further work
options.C: Deletes all scratchpad files to free memory
options.D: Restarts the agent process to clear all state

questions.explanation: /compact summarizes the accumulated conversation history into a condensed representation, freeing context window tokens while preserving the essential semantic content of the session. This allows the agent to continue working without losing the thread of the exploration.

whyWrongMap.A: /compact operates on the context window, not on disk files; it has no effect on source code file sizes.
whyWrongMap.C: Scratchpad files are persistent storage and are not deleted by /compact; they are intentionally kept to survive exactly this kind of compaction.
whyWrongMap.D: Restarting would lose all in-memory state; /compact is a non-destructive operation that preserves semantic continuity.

references: /compact command to summarize and free context

questions.scenario: You are orchestrating an exploration of a 200,000-line codebase. You need to: (1) map all API endpoints, (2) document database schema relationships, and (3) trace the authentication middleware chain. Each task requires reading 20–40 files.
questions.text: Which approach best manages context while completing all three tasks?

options.A: Complete all three tasks sequentially in a single agent session, reading files as needed
options.B: Spawn three focused subagents in parallel, each tackling one task and writing findings to separate scratchpad files
options.C: Use /compact after each task to free context, then continue in the same agent
options.D: Read all 40–120 files upfront to build complete context before beginning any analysis

questions.explanation: Spawning focused subagents in parallel is ideal when tasks are independent and each requires substantial file reading. Each subagent has a fresh, focused context window dedicated to its specific task, eliminating cross-contamination between concerns. Writing to scratchpad files ensures findings persist and can be aggregated by the orchestrator.

whyWrongMap.A: A single sequential session will accumulate context from all three tasks, causing degradation by the time the third task begins and making it hard to maintain precision across all findings.
whyWrongMap.C: Using /compact between tasks risks losing nuance from earlier tasks; subagent delegation is preferred when tasks are independent and substantial enough to justify fresh context windows.
whyWrongMap.D: Reading all files upfront maximally pollutes the context window before any analysis begins, almost guaranteeing context exhaustion and degraded reasoning quality.

references: Subagent delegation: spawn focused subagents for specific tasks

questions.scenario: An agent needs to find all usages of a deprecated `legacyAuth()` function across a TypeScript codebase with 500 files. The agent has already used 60% of its context window on earlier exploration tasks.
questions.text: What is the most context-efficient approach to locate all usages of `legacyAuth()`?

options.A: Use Glob to find all `.ts` files, then read each one to check for the function name
options.B: Use Grep with the pattern `legacyAuth` scoped to TypeScript files to get only matching file paths and line numbers
options.C: Ask the user to manually search the codebase and report back
options.D: Read the entire codebase into context and then scan for the function name

questions.explanation: Grep in `files_with_matches` or `content` mode returns only matching lines, consuming a tiny fraction of the context that reading full files would require. With 60% context already used, targeted Grep is the only responsible approach — it finds exactly what is needed without loading irrelevant file content.

whyWrongMap.A: Globbing and then reading each file individually is highly inefficient — it reads entire file contents for most files that won't contain the target function, wasting the remaining 40% of context.
whyWrongMap.C: Asking the user to search manually defeats the purpose of agent-driven exploration and is not a valid context management strategy.
whyWrongMap.D: Reading the entire codebase at 60% context usage would immediately cause context overflow, making this approach impossible and contrary to avoiding context pollution.

references: Incremental understanding: Glob → Read → Grep workflow
Avoiding context pollution from large file reads

questions.scenario: You are deciding whether to explore a complex data transformation pipeline directly in the current agent session or delegate it to a subagent. The pipeline spans 15 files, you have already explored the surrounding domain, and the insights from the surrounding domain are likely needed to interpret the pipeline.
questions.text: Based on the described scenario, should you explore directly or delegate to a subagent, and why?

options.A: Delegate to a subagent, because subagents are always more efficient for file exploration
options.B: Explore directly in the current session, because the existing domain context is valuable for interpreting the pipeline and would be lost in a fresh subagent
options.C: Delegate to a subagent and pass the full context window as input so it has all the background
options.D: Always use /compact first, then delegate to a subagent regardless of existing context

questions.explanation: The key decision criterion is whether existing context adds value to the new task. When domain knowledge already in the context window is directly relevant to interpreting the target files, exploring directly preserves that cross-cutting insight. Subagent delegation is optimal for independent tasks where fresh context is an advantage, not a disadvantage.

whyWrongMap.A: Subagents are not universally more efficient; the correct choice depends on whether the task is independent of existing context. When existing context is load-bearing, delegation loses that advantage.
whyWrongMap.C: Passing the full context window to a subagent negates the benefit of delegation (fresh focused context) and is architecturally unsound — subagents should receive concise, scoped instructions.
whyWrongMap.D: Blindly applying /compact and delegating ignores the situational analysis that should drive this decision; sometimes continuing in the same session is the right choice.

questions.scenario: An agent has been exploring a Node.js backend and has written detailed scratchpad notes about route handlers and middleware. The context window is now at 75% and a new request arrives to analyze the React frontend codebase, which is entirely independent.
questions.text: What is the optimal sequence of actions before beginning the frontend analysis?

options.A: Begin reading frontend files immediately to maximize session continuity
options.B: Run /compact to summarize the backend findings, then continue with frontend analysis in the same session
options.C: Verify scratchpad files contain complete backend findings, then run /compact or spawn a fresh subagent for the frontend analysis
options.D: Delete the scratchpad files to free disk space, then start fresh

questions.explanation: Before compacting or switching tasks, you must verify that scratchpad files contain all critical findings — /compact may discard nuance that hasn't been externalized yet. Once findings are safely persisted, /compact or a fresh subagent for the independent frontend task are both valid options, with the choice depending on context levels and task independence.

whyWrongMap.A: Proceeding immediately at 75% context with an entirely new domain risks exhausting context mid-analysis and potentially corrupting findings from both the backend and frontend tasks.
whyWrongMap.B: Running /compact without first verifying scratchpad completeness risks losing backend findings that haven't been written to files yet; the verification step is mandatory.
whyWrongMap.D: Deleting scratchpad files destroys the persisted findings that are the entire point of the scratchpad strategy; this is the opposite of correct behavior.

references: Scratchpad files: write findings to files so they survive context resets

questions.scenario: A subagent has been tasked with exploring a microservices architecture. It needs to understand 8 independent service directories, each containing 30–50 files with their own domain logic.
questions.text: How should the subagent structure its exploration to minimize context degradation?

options.A: Read all files from all 8 services before beginning any analysis
options.B: Explore all 8 services simultaneously by interleaving file reads
options.C: Explore one service at a time, write findings for each service to a scratchpad file before moving to the next, using Glob → Read → Grep per service
options.D: Spawn 8 further sub-subagents, one per service, and have them report back simultaneously

questions.explanation: Systematic service-by-service exploration with incremental scratchpad writing is the correct pattern. Each service gets focused attention using the Glob → Read → Grep workflow, and findings are externalized to files before the next service begins. This prevents cross-service context pollution and ensures no findings are lost as context fills up.

whyWrongMap.A: Reading all files upfront from all 8 services immediately saturates the context window before any analysis can begin, making this approach unworkable.
whyWrongMap.B: Interleaving reads across services pollutes context with mixed concerns, making it harder to reason about any individual service and increasing the risk of confusion between similar patterns.
whyWrongMap.D: While further delegation is theoretically possible, spawning 8 sub-subagents adds orchestration overhead and complexity; the single-subagent incremental approach is sufficient and more practical for this scale.

questions.scenario: An architect agent is orchestrating a full system audit of a 500,000-line codebase. The audit has three phases: (1) dependency graph construction requiring broad file scanning, (2) security vulnerability analysis requiring deep reading of auth and crypto modules, and (3) performance bottleneck identification requiring profiling data correlation. The architect has limited context budget and must ensure findings from all three phases are synthesized into a final report.
questions.text: Which orchestration strategy best balances context efficiency, finding quality, and synthesis capability?

options.A: Run all three phases sequentially in the orchestrator's context, using /compact between phases
options.B: Delegate all three phases to a single subagent with a large context window
options.C: Spawn three parallel specialized subagents, each writing structured findings to named scratchpad files, then have the orchestrator synthesize by reading only the scratchpad files
options.D: Have the orchestrator read all 500,000 lines itself to maintain full context for synthesis

questions.explanation: This is the canonical multi-agent context management pattern. Parallel specialized subagents each have fresh, focused context windows optimized for their specific task domain. Writing structured findings to named scratchpad files creates a clean interface between exploration and synthesis. The orchestrator only reads the compact scratchpad outputs — not raw source files — keeping its synthesis context clean and high-signal. This pattern scales to arbitrarily large codebases.

whyWrongMap.A: Sequential execution with /compact between phases loses the ability to perform cross-phase synthesis within the same context; /compact may discard findings needed for final synthesis, and sequential execution is slower than parallel.
whyWrongMap.B: A single subagent with a large context window handling all three phases still suffers from cross-domain context pollution between phases, and does not benefit from parallel execution or specialization.
whyWrongMap.D: Reading 500,000 lines directly is impossible within any current context window and represents the most extreme form of context pollution; this option is architecturally invalid.

references: Subagent delegation: spawn focused subagents for specific tasks

questions.scenario: During a long exploration session, an agent used /compact and then realized that a critical architectural insight about a circular dependency — discovered 2 hours earlier — was lost in the compaction summary. The insight had not been written to any scratchpad file because the agent was mid-analysis when /compact was run.
questions.text: Which combination of practices would have prevented this information loss?

options.A: Using a larger context window model to avoid needing /compact
options.B: Writing findings to scratchpad files immediately upon discovery, before context accumulates to the point where /compact becomes necessary
options.C: Running /compact more frequently so each compaction summary is smaller and more detailed
options.D: Avoiding /compact entirely and always spawning a new agent session when context is full

questions.explanation: The failure mode here is relying on /compact to preserve in-progress findings. /compact is a lossy compression — it preserves semantic summaries, not complete analytical details. The correct discipline is to externalize findings to scratchpad files eagerly and continuously, treating the context window as volatile working memory and files as durable storage. This way, /compact can run safely at any time without risk of information loss.

whyWrongMap.A: A larger context window delays but does not eliminate the problem; eventually all context windows fill, and the same information loss occurs if the scratchpad discipline is absent. This is an architectural patch, not a solution.
whyWrongMap.C: More frequent /compact runs result in more compaction events, each of which can lose in-progress findings; without the scratchpad discipline, this increases rather than decreases risk of loss.
whyWrongMap.D: Avoiding /compact entirely and spawning new sessions still results in information loss unless findings are externalized to files — the new session starts with no memory of prior discoveries.

references: Scratchpad files: write findings to files so they survive context resets

options.C: Use targeted Grep to find cross-service interface points and constructor signatures without reading full service implementations, leveraging existing payment domain knowledge to interpret results
options.D: Run /compact immediately to free context, then delegate to a subagent

questions.explanation: This scenario requires nuanced judgment. The existing payment domain knowledge is directly relevant to interpreting integration patterns, arguing against delegation. However, 40% context is insufficient to read all 15–30 service files. The optimal approach uses targeted Grep to extract only the interface-level information (method signatures, public API, dependency injection points) needed to assess integration correctness, without reading full implementations. This leverages existing context knowledge while staying within context bounds.

whyWrongMap.A: Delegating based solely on a 40% context threshold ignores the value of existing payment domain knowledge that is directly relevant to the analysis; the delegate decision should be based on task independence, not an arbitrary context percentage.
whyWrongMap.B: Reading all files from all three services at 40% remaining context would almost certainly cause context exhaustion mid-analysis, producing incomplete and potentially misleading findings.
whyWrongMap.D: Running /compact before exploring risks losing the payment domain knowledge that makes direct exploration superior to delegation in this case; the timing of /compact must account for what existing context is worth preserving.

questions.scenario: A team is designing the context management strategy for a Claude-based code review agent that will autonomously review pull requests in a repository with 300,000 lines of code. questions.text: Which architectural design best handles the full range of PR sizes while maintaining review quality?

options.B: Always spawn a dedicated subagent per PR regardless of size
options.C: Implement a tiered strategy: for small PRs use direct exploration; for large PRs spawn specialized subagents per concern (diff analysis, pattern consistency, test coverage), with all agents writing findings to structured scratchpad files for orchestrator synthesis

Large PRs (5,000 lines) with multi-concern analysis benefit from parallel specialized subagents, each with a fresh focused context window, converging on scratchpad files for orchestrator synthesis. This design scales gracefully, avoids unnecessary overhead for simple cases, and maintains quality for complex cases.

whyWrongMap.A: A single-session approach with /compact is inadequate for large PRs where the diff itself plus surrounding context may exceed context window limits even after compaction; quality degrades unpredictably.
whyWrongMap.B: Always spawning a dedicated subagent per PR regardless of size adds unnecessary orchestration overhead for trivially small PRs and fails to address the multi-concern decomposition needed for large PRs.
