questions.scenario: A document-processing pipeline assigns each extracted document a single confidence score (e.g., 0.72) before deciding whether to send it for human review. A senior architect suggests switching to field-level confidence instead.
questions.text: What is the primary advantage of field-level confidence scoring over document-level confidence scoring?

options.A: It reduces the total number of documents that require any human review.
options.B: It allows reviewers to focus only on the specific fields that are uncertain rather than re-examining the entire document.
options.C: It eliminates the need for a review queue because each field can auto-approve independently.
options.D: It standardises confidence values across all document types so calibration is simpler.

questions.explanation: Field-level confidence (e.g., 'name: high, date: low') isolates uncertainty to the precise fields that need attention. Reviewers can then inspect and correct only those low-confidence fields rather than re-processing the whole document, which saves time and reduces cognitive load. Document-level scores aggregate uncertainty and can mask which specific fields are problematic.

whyWrongMap.A: Field-level confidence does not inherently reduce the number of documents reviewed; it may actually route more documents to review because any low-confidence field triggers review. The benefit is targeted effort, not volume reduction.
whyWrongMap.C: Low-confidence fields still require review; field-level granularity directs reviewers to the right place but does not eliminate the need for human oversight of uncertain extractions.
whyWrongMap.D: Field-level confidence increases calibration complexity because each field type may have a different accuracy distribution; it does not standardise values across document types.

questions.scenario: A team is designing a review queue for an invoice-extraction system. They want to ensure the highest-risk items are reviewed first so that errors with large financial impact are caught before downstream processing.
questions.text: Which two factors should primarily drive review queue prioritisation?

options.A: Document file size and the time it took the model to generate the extraction.
options.B: Model confidence score and the business impact of an error in that document.
options.C: The number of fields extracted and the length of the document.
options.D: The model version used and whether the document was processed in batch or real-time mode.

questions.explanation: Review queue priority should reflect both how uncertain the model is (confidence score) and how costly an error would be (business impact). A low-confidence extraction for a high-value invoice should jump to the front of the queue, while a low-confidence extraction for a trivial record may wait. Combining these two dimensions ensures reviewer effort has maximum protective value.

whyWrongMap.A: File size and processing time are operational metrics with no direct relationship to error risk or business impact; they should not drive prioritisation.
whyWrongMap.C: Field count and document length are structural properties, not indicators of extraction accuracy or downstream cost of errors.
whyWrongMap.D: Model version and processing mode have no consistent relationship to the urgency of human review; a newer model processed in batch can still produce a high-impact error.

questions.scenario: During a post-deployment audit, the team discovers that the model claims 90% confidence on a class of medical-record fields, but actual accuracy on those fields is only 65%. The model is consistently overconfident.
questions.text: What does this discrepancy between stated confidence and actual accuracy indicate?

options.A: The model has a calibration problem and its confidence scores cannot be trusted at face value.
options.B: The review threshold is set too low, causing too many correct extractions to be sent for review.
options.C: The model is under-performing due to insufficient context window size.
options.D: The human reviewers are introducing errors that lower the measured accuracy.

questions.explanation: Calibration measures whether a model's stated confidence matches its actual accuracy. If the model claims 90% confidence but is only 65% accurate, it is overconfident and poorly calibrated. This means the confidence scores should not be used directly for auto-approve thresholds without recalibration — for example, applying Platt scaling or isotonic regression to map raw scores to true probabilities.

whyWrongMap.B: A threshold set too low would send more items to review than necessary, but it would not explain why stated confidence exceeds actual accuracy — that is a model calibration issue, not a threshold issue.
whyWrongMap.C: Context window size affects the model's ability to process long inputs, not the relationship between its stated confidence and ground-truth accuracy on extracted fields.
whyWrongMap.D: The scenario describes measured accuracy (ground truth comparison), which accounts for reviewer corrections; it is a property of the model's output quality, not reviewer error.

questions.scenario: An architect is defining the auto-approve policy for a contract-extraction system. Most extracted clauses are low-risk boilerplate, but a small subset involve liability caps and indemnification language that carry significant legal weight.
questions.text: What is the most appropriate criterion for requiring mandatory human review rather than auto-approving an extraction?

options.A: The extraction took more than two seconds to produce.
options.B: The document is longer than ten pages.
options.C: The confidence score falls below a defined threshold OR the field type carries high business or legal impact.
options.D: The user who submitted the document does not have administrator privileges.

questions.explanation: Human review should be required whenever confidence is low (indicating model uncertainty) OR when the field's impact is high regardless of confidence. Even a high-confidence extraction of a liability cap clause warrants review because the cost of an undetected error is severe. Combining both criteria ensures that neither uncertain nor high-stakes extractions slip through without oversight.

whyWrongMap.A: Processing latency is an infrastructure metric with no bearing on extraction accuracy or the downstream risk of an error.
whyWrongMap.B: Document length may correlate loosely with complexity but is not a reliable proxy for extraction accuracy or business impact of specific fields.
whyWrongMap.D: User privilege level is an access-control concern, not a signal about extraction quality or field risk; it should not drive the review decision.

questions.scenario: A pipeline processes three document types: invoices (60% of volume, 94% model accuracy), contracts (30% of volume, 78% accuracy), and purchase orders (10% of volume, 88% accuracy). The team has a fixed weekly budget of 500 manual reviews. They want to allocate review effort to maximise error detection.
questions.text: Which review strategy best allocates the 500 manual reviews across document types?

options.A: Distribute reviews proportionally to volume: 300 invoices, 150 contracts, 50 purchase orders.
options.B: Review all documents randomly regardless of type, since random sampling is unbiased.
options.C: Concentrate all 500 reviews on contracts because they have the lowest accuracy.
options.D: Allocate reviews inversely proportional to accuracy, weighting toward document types with higher error rates while maintaining coverage of all types.

questions.explanation: Accuracy tracking by document type should inform where review effort goes. Contracts have a 22% error rate — more than double the invoice rate — so they deserve a disproportionately large share of the review budget. However, completely ignoring invoices and purchase orders leaves blind spots. The correct approach is accuracy-weighted allocation: more reviews where accuracy is lowest, but some baseline coverage of every document type. This is distinct from simple proportional (volume-based) allocation which ignores accuracy differences.

whyWrongMap.A: Volume-proportional allocation ignores that contracts are far less accurate than invoices; it wastes most of the budget on document types that rarely produce errors.
whyWrongMap.B: Pure random sampling is unbiased but inefficient: it allocates reviews in proportion to volume, which — like option A — underweights the high-error contract type.
whyWrongMap.C: Concentrating all reviews on contracts catches contract errors but provides zero ongoing monitoring of invoices and purchase orders, creating undetected drift risk in those categories.

questions.scenario: A team uses stratified sampling to draw 200 documents for weekly accuracy audits. Their confidence buckets are: low (0–0.5, 15% of volume), medium (0.5–0.8, 50% of volume), and high (0.8–1.0, 35% of volume). They want the sample to reflect the distribution of each bucket.
questions.text: How many documents should be sampled from each confidence bucket in a proportional stratified sample of 200?

options.A: Low: 67, Medium: 67, High: 66 (equal allocation across buckets).
options.B: Low: 30, Medium: 100, High: 70 (proportional to bucket volume).
options.C: Low: 100, Medium: 70, High: 30 (inverse-proportional to bucket volume).
options.D: Low: 200, Medium: 0, High: 0 (only sample uncertain documents).

questions.explanation: Proportional stratified sampling draws from each stratum in proportion to that stratum's share of the total population. With 15% low, 50% medium, and 35% high: 0.15 × 200 = 30 low, 0.50 × 200 = 100 medium, 0.35 × 200 = 70 high. This ensures the sample mirrors the actual confidence distribution, giving unbiased accuracy estimates for each bucket and for the overall system.

whyWrongMap.A: Equal allocation (roughly 67 each) oversamples the low-confidence bucket and undersamples the medium bucket relative to their actual proportions, introducing selection bias into accuracy estimates.
whyWrongMap.C: Inverse-proportional allocation deliberately oversamples rare or uncertain buckets — this is a valid technique for error discovery but is not proportional stratified sampling; it would bias overall accuracy estimates upward.
whyWrongMap.D: Sampling only the low-confidence bucket provides no information about accuracy in the medium or high buckets, making it impossible to validate whether the auto-approve threshold is appropriate.

questions.scenario: After six months in production, an architect reviews calibration data for a claims-processing model. The model's confidence scores in the 0.9–1.0 bucket correspond to an actual accuracy of 91%, but scores in the 0.6–0.7 bucket correspond to only 48% actual accuracy. The auto-approve threshold is currently 0.65.
questions.text: What should the architect do to address this calibration gap?

options.A: Lower the auto-approve threshold to 0.5 so fewer documents are auto-approved.
options.B: Apply a post-hoc calibration technique (e.g., Platt scaling or isotonic regression) to remap raw scores to calibrated probabilities, then set the auto-approve threshold on the calibrated scale.
options.C: Disable confidence-based routing and require human review for all documents.
options.D: Increase the auto-approve threshold to 0.9 to match the bucket where accuracy aligns with confidence.

questions.explanation: The root cause is that raw model confidence scores are not reliable probability estimates — the model is well-calibrated at high confidence but severely overconfident in the 0.6–0.7 range. Post-hoc calibration methods such as Platt scaling (logistic regression on held-out outputs) or isotonic regression transform raw scores into calibrated probabilities where, e.g., a score of 0.65 truly reflects ~65% accuracy. Once calibrated, the team can set thresholds with confidence that the scores mean what they say.

whyWrongMap.A: Lowering the threshold to 0.5 sends more documents to review but does not fix the underlying miscalibration; the team would still have no reliable mapping between scores and actual accuracy, leading to arbitrary threshold choices.
whyWrongMap.C: Requiring review for all documents is safe but defeats the purpose of automated extraction; it is a fallback, not a calibration solution.
whyWrongMap.D: Raising the threshold to 0.9 reduces auto-approval volume drastically and shifts the workload to reviewers without addressing why lower-confidence scores are miscalibrated; it is a workaround that ignores the calibration problem.

questions.scenario: A healthcare document pipeline extracts patient demographics, diagnoses, and billing codes. The compliance team mandates that any extraction that will directly update a patient record without clinician review must meet a strict accuracy standard. The model reports field-level confidence for each extracted value.
questions.text: Which auto-approve policy correctly applies the 'when to require human review vs auto-approve' principle for this regulated context?

options.A: Auto-approve all fields where model confidence exceeds 0.75, regardless of field type.
options.B: Require human review for all fields unconditionally because the domain is regulated.
options.C: Auto-approve low-impact demographics fields above a high confidence threshold (e.g., ≥0.95), but require human review for all diagnosis and billing-code fields due to clinical and financial risk.
options.D: Auto-approve if the document-level average confidence exceeds 0.80, ignoring individual field confidence.

questions.explanation: In regulated healthcare contexts, the auto-approve vs. review decision should be stratified by both field-level confidence and field-level impact. Demographic fields (name, address) carry lower clinical risk and can be auto-approved at a high confidence threshold. Diagnosis and billing codes carry direct clinical and financial consequences — errors can harm patients or trigger fraud scrutiny — so they warrant mandatory review regardless of confidence. This tiered policy minimises reviewer workload without compromising safety on high-impact fields.

whyWrongMap.A: A uniform 0.75 threshold ignores field-type risk; a 0.76-confidence diagnosis code could be auto-approved and directly harm a patient without any clinician seeing it.
whyWrongMap.B: Universal review is maximally safe but operationally unsustainable at scale and defeats the efficiency goal of automated extraction; it is not a 'design' — it is the absence of one.
whyWrongMap.D: Document-level average confidence masks field-level variance: a document could average 0.85 while containing a critically miscategorised diagnosis at 0.55 confidence, which would be auto-approved under this policy.

questions.scenario: A team tracks accuracy over time for their document extraction pipeline. After a model update three weeks ago, they observe that accuracy on 'contract expiry date' fields dropped from 93% to 71%, while all other fields remain stable. The model's confidence scores on this field have not changed.
questions.text: What does the divergence between stable confidence scores and falling accuracy on 'contract expiry date' fields indicate, and what action should the team take?

options.A: The drop is within normal statistical variance; no action is needed.
options.B: The model update introduced a regression on date parsing for this field type, and the confidence scores are not capturing this degradation — the team should lower the auto-approve threshold for date fields and trigger retraining or rollback.
options.C: Human reviewers are making more errors on date fields, causing the measured accuracy to fall.
options.D: The accuracy tracking system is miscalculating accuracy for date fields due to a date-format mismatch.

questions.explanation: When accuracy drops on a specific field type after a model change but confidence scores remain unchanged, it signals a calibration regression: the model's self-reported confidence no longer tracks actual performance for that field. Accuracy tracking by document type and field type is precisely designed to catch this pattern. The immediate response is to lower the auto-approve threshold for contract date fields (routing more to human review) and investigate the model update for a regression in date parsing, with retraining or rollback as remediation.

whyWrongMap.A: A 22-percentage-point drop that correlates with a model update and persists for three weeks is not statistical noise; dismissing it risks a large backlog of incorrect date extractions propagating into contracts.
whyWrongMap.C: The scenario specifies that accuracy is measured against ground truth, not reviewer decisions; reviewer error would not systematically lower measured accuracy unless reviewers are the ground-truth source, which is not indicated.
whyWrongMap.D: While a format mismatch is worth investigating, the scenario's correlation with the model update and stability of all other fields points to a model regression rather than a measurement artefact.

questions.scenario: An architect is designing the calibration pipeline for a multi-class document extraction system. The model outputs a softmax probability vector across 50 possible values for each field. Calibration analysis reveals that the model is well-calibrated on common values (seen >10,000 times in training) but severely overconfident on rare values (seen <200 times). The team must set auto-approve thresholds.
questions.text: Which calibration strategy best addresses the per-class confidence reliability disparity without discarding the model's useful signal on common values?

options.A: Apply a single global Platt scaling transformation across all 50 classes using a single held-out validation set.
options.B: Apply temperature scaling globally to the logits — a single scalar parameter tuned on a held-out set — which corrects average calibration but may leave rare-class overconfidence partially unresolved; supplement with a rare-class auto-approve blacklist.
options.C: Raise the global auto-approve threshold to 0.99 to ensure only the most confident predictions are approved, regardless of class frequency.
options.D: Disable confidence scoring entirely for rare values and require manual review of all 50-class field extractions.

questions.explanation: Temperature scaling is a simple, single-parameter post-hoc calibration method that divides logits by a learned temperature T, softening the probability distribution. It preserves the rank order of predictions and improves average calibration efficiently. However, for rare classes where training data is sparse, per-class miscalibration may persist after global temperature scaling. Supplementing with a rare-class auto-approve blacklist (requiring review for any prediction in a low-frequency class regardless of confidence) directly addresses the residual overconfidence without discarding the model's reliable signal on common values.

whyWrongMap.A: A single global Platt scaling fit on a pooled validation set will be dominated by common classes; rare-class calibration will remain poor because there are too few rare-class examples to influence the logistic regression parameters meaningfully.
whyWrongMap.C: A 0.99 global threshold drastically reduces auto-approval rates across all classes, punishing well-calibrated common-value predictions unnecessarily; it is a blunt instrument that does not distinguish between reliable and unreliable confidence regions.
whyWrongMap.D: Disabling confidence scoring for rare values and requiring manual review for all 50-class fields abandons the efficiency benefit of the model entirely for the majority of extractions where the model is well-calibrated.

questions.scenario: A financial services firm processes 50,000 trade-confirmation documents per day. Each document has 12 fields. Human review capacity is 2,000 field-reviews per day. Current pipeline: all 600,000 daily field extractions are scored with field-level confidence. The team must design a review workflow that maximises error detection within the review capacity constraint, given the following data: field accuracy at confidence ≥0.90 is 97%; at 0.70–0.89 is 84%; at <0.70 is 61%. Roughly 5% of field extractions fall below 0.70, 20% fall in 0.70–0.89, and 75% fall at ≥0.90.
questions.text: Which review workflow design maximises expected error detection within the 2,000 field-review daily capacity?

options.A: Review a random sample of 2,000 fields from the full daily population.
options.B: Review all fields in the <0.70 confidence bucket (30,000 fields) — this exceeds capacity, so triage by the highest-impact documents first until 2,000 reviews are consumed.
options.C: Allocate all 2,000 daily reviews to the <0.70 confidence bucket using stratified sampling within that bucket, weighted by business impact.
options.D: Split reviews proportionally across buckets by volume: 75 reviews on ≥0.90 fields, 400 on 0.70–0.89 fields, and 100 on <0.70 fields, scaled to 2,000 total.

questions.explanation: Expected errors detected = reviews × error rate. The <0.70 bucket has a 39% error rate (100% − 61%), versus 16% for the mid bucket and 3% for the high bucket. Concentrating all 2,000 reviews in the lowest-confidence bucket maximises expected error yield: 2,000 × 0.39 = 780 errors caught, versus 2,000 × 0.03 = 60 if reviews were spread proportionally. Within the <0.70 bucket, weighting by business impact (e.g., high-value trade amounts first) further increases the value of each review. The 30,000 low-confidence fields exceed capacity, so stratified sampling by impact within the bucket is the correct sub-selection method.

whyWrongMap.A: Random sampling across all 600,000 fields gives roughly 75% of reviews on the high-confidence bucket (error rate 3%), yielding an expected ~78 errors detected — dramatically lower than concentrating reviews on the low-confidence bucket.
whyWrongMap.B: Attempting to review all 30,000 low-confidence fields and triaging by impact is equivalent to option C but described imprecisely; the correct framing is to sample 2,000 from the <0.70 bucket weighted by impact, not to attempt all 30,000 and stop at capacity.
whyWrongMap.D: Proportional-to-volume allocation wastes 75% of review capacity (1,500 reviews) on the ≥0.90 bucket where only ~3% of extractions are wrong, yielding ~45 errors from those reviews versus ~780 if the same budget were spent on the <0.70 bucket.

questions.scenario: An architect is asked to design a feedback loop that uses reviewer corrections to continuously improve model calibration. The system currently collects reviewer decisions (accept / correct) alongside the model's original confidence scores. A data scientist proposes using this data directly to retrain the confidence head of the model weekly. A second proposal is to use the reviewer data only for post-hoc recalibration (isotonic regression) without touching model weights.
questions.text: Which design concern most critically distinguishes the two proposals and should drive the architectural decision?

options.A: Retraining model weights weekly introduces the risk of distribution shift from reviewer bias, where systematic reviewer errors or selection bias in the review queue (only low-confidence items are reviewed) corrupt the training signal; post-hoc recalibration on held-out data avoids weight changes but requires a representative sample of all confidence levels.
options.B: Post-hoc recalibration is always preferred because it is computationally cheaper than retraining.
options.C: Weekly retraining is always preferred because it allows the model to learn from corrections faster.
options.D: The two proposals are equivalent in practice because confidence calibration and model accuracy are independent properties.

questions.explanation: The critical concern is selection bias and feedback loop corruption. Because only low-confidence items go to reviewers, the correction dataset is not representative of the full confidence distribution — the model never receives feedback on high-confidence errors. Using this biased dataset to retrain model weights can introduce systematic distortions. Additionally, if reviewers make consistent errors (e.g., always accepting a wrong value for an uncommon entity), those errors pollute the training set. Post-hoc isotonic regression calibration on a representative held-out set (including stratified high-confidence samples) sidesteps the weight-corruption risk while still improving score reliability. The architectural decision must acknowledge these asymmetric risks.

whyWrongMap.B: Computational cost is a real consideration but is not the critical architectural concern; a wrong but cheap calibration is worse than a correct but expensive one. The critical issue is data representativeness and feedback loop integrity.
whyWrongMap.C: Speed of learning is secondary to correctness of the training signal; fast retraining on a biased or reviewer-corrupted dataset can degrade the model faster than no retraining at all.
whyWrongMap.D: Confidence calibration and model accuracy are not independent: retraining on reviewer corrections changes both the model's feature representations and its confidence head simultaneously, and a miscalibrated confidence head that incorrectly signals high confidence on wrong predictions is a direct accuracy risk.

questions.scenario: A government agency deploys a document-classification pipeline that routes benefit applications to one of five processing tracks. Field-level confidence is available. Regulatory requirements mandate that any classification error affecting a citizen's benefit eligibility must be detectable within 30 days. The system processes 10,000 applications per day. Human review staff can review 500 applications per day. The team must design a monitoring and review workflow that satisfies the regulatory detectability requirement.
questions.text: Which combination of strategies best satisfies the 30-day detectability requirement while operating within the 500-review-per-day staff constraint?

options.A: Route all 10,000 daily applications to human review and hire additional staff to meet capacity.
options.B: Use confidence-based auto-approve for high-confidence applications, route low-confidence applications to the daily review queue, and implement a stratified random audit (sampling proportionally from all confidence buckets daily) to provide statistical detectability coverage across auto-approved applications — sizing the audit sample so that a 1% error rate in the auto-approved tier would be detected within 30 days at 95% confidence.
options.C: Auto-approve all applications where document-level confidence exceeds 0.85, and review the remaining 15% within daily capacity, with no further monitoring of auto-approved applications.
options.D: Implement a 30-day rolling review where every application processed on day 1 is reviewed on day 30, creating a one-month lag review cycle.

questions.explanation: Meeting a 30-day statistical detectability requirement requires both operational review (catching low-confidence cases in near-real-time) and systematic audit sampling of the auto-approved tier. Without auditing high-confidence auto-approvals, errors in that tier are invisible until a citizen complains. The stratified audit uses the daily review budget for a dual purpose: reviewing urgent low-confidence cases AND sampling across the confidence distribution to maintain statistical guarantees. Sample sizing (using statistical power calculations) ensures a minimum detectable error rate within the 30-day window. This is the only option that satisfies both the capacity constraint and the regulatory detectability mandate through principled design.

whyWrongMap.A: Routing all 10,000 applications to human review requires 20× the available staff capacity; it is operationally infeasible and does not represent a design — it is an admission that no automated system is being used.
whyWrongMap.C: Auto-approving the 85% high-confidence tier with no further monitoring creates a blind spot: errors in that tier are undetectable until downstream impact occurs, which may violate the 30-day detectability requirement if no systematic audit exists.
whyWrongMap.D: A 30-day rolling review where every application is re-examined one month later would require reviewing 10,000 applications per day (one month's backlog) on top of daily new intake — far exceeding the 500-review capacity — and introduces a systematic one-month delay before any error is caught, exactly at the regulatory limit.

questions.scenario: A Claude-based research assistant synthesizes information from three articles. One article states a drug was approved in 2021, a second says 2022, and the third does not mention a date. The assistant outputs: 'The drug was approved in 2022.'
questions.text: What provenance principle does this response violate?

options.A: Temporal data handling — the assistant should note when each source's information was current
options.B: Conflict annotation — the assistant silently picked one source instead of surfacing the disagreement between sources
options.C: Citation tracking — the assistant failed to include hyperlinks to the original articles
options.D: Uncertainty expression — the assistant should have refused to answer due to conflicting data

questions.explanation: When two sources provide conflicting factual claims, the correct behavior is to surface the conflict explicitly (conflict annotation) rather than silently choosing one value. The response should say something like 'Source A states 2021, Source B states 2022' so the user can evaluate the discrepancy.

whyWrongMap.A: Temporal data handling concerns flagging when information was current or has become stale over time — it is not the primary issue when two sources give different values for the same fact.
whyWrongMap.C: Citation tracking is about maintaining source links through synthesis chains; while important, the immediate violation here is picking one of two conflicting values without disclosure.
whyWrongMap.D: Refusing to answer is not required — expressing uncertainty by presenting both values is preferred over refusal, which would deprive the user of the available information.

questions.scenario: A developer asks Claude to summarize a market report. The report was published in January 2023, and the summary will be used in December 2025. Claude produces a summary without mentioning when the underlying data was collected.
questions.text: Which information provenance practice is missing from this summary?

options.A: Claim-source mapping for every statistic
options.B: Conflict annotation between competing market forecasts
options.C: Flagging that the data is from January 2023 and may be stale relative to current conditions
options.D: Expressing uncertainty by listing all possible market outcomes

questions.explanation: Temporal data handling requires noting when the underlying information was current and flagging data that may have become stale. A summary used two years after the source was published should clearly state the data's publication date so consumers can assess its relevance.

whyWrongMap.A: Claim-source mapping is important but is a separate concern; the scenario does not indicate that individual claims are unattributed — the primary gap is the missing temporal context.
whyWrongMap.B: Conflict annotation applies when multiple sources disagree; the scenario describes a single report, so no inter-source conflict exists to annotate.
whyWrongMap.D: Listing all market outcomes is not a provenance practice; uncertainty expression means disclosing the source and its limitations, not enumerating every possible scenario.

questions.scenario: A Claude pipeline performs three sequential summarization steps: raw documents → intermediate summary → final brief. By the final step, no source identifiers remain in the output.
questions.text: What term describes the failure mode illustrated in this pipeline?

options.A: Citation drift
options.B: Provenance loss in summarization chains
options.C: Temporal degradation
options.D: Conflict collapse

questions.explanation: Provenance loss in summarization chains describes the progressive disappearance of source attribution as information passes through multiple synthesis or summarization steps. Each step can strip context until the final output is completely disconnected from its origins.

whyWrongMap.A: 'Citation drift' is not a standard information provenance term; while it evokes the idea, the recognized term for this failure mode is provenance loss in summarization chains.
whyWrongMap.C: 'Temporal degradation' refers to data becoming outdated over time, not to the loss of source information during processing.
whyWrongMap.D: 'Conflict collapse' is not a standard term; it might suggest conflicts being hidden, but the scenario describes source attribution disappearing rather than conflicting claims being merged.

questions.scenario: A Claude-powered assistant is asked 'What is the current population of Tokyo?' Claude's training data has a figure from 2021, but the question is being asked in 2025.
questions.text: What is the most information-provenance-sound response pattern?

options.A: State the 2021 figure as fact without qualification
options.B: Refuse to answer because the data might be outdated
options.C: Provide the 2021 figure, note it comes from training data current as of that year, and recommend verifying with a current source
options.D: Provide the figure and add a generic disclaimer that all AI responses may be incorrect

questions.explanation: Temporal data handling requires noting when the information was current and flagging potentially stale data. Providing the figure with its temporal context (2021 training data) and a recommendation to verify satisfies both usefulness and provenance transparency.

whyWrongMap.A: Stating the figure without qualification violates temporal data handling by presenting stale data as if it were current, which can mislead users.
whyWrongMap.B: Refusal is disproportionate; the correct approach is to share the available data with appropriate temporal caveats, not to withhold it entirely.
whyWrongMap.D: A generic AI disclaimer does not constitute information provenance practice — it does not identify the specific source, its vintage, or what to do about the gap.

questions.scenario: An architect is designing a multi-agent RAG system where Agent A retrieves documents, Agent B extracts claims, and Agent C writes the final report. The product owner requires that every claim in the final report can be traced back to its original document.
questions.text: Which architectural pattern best ensures end-to-end claim-source mapping across all three agents?

options.A: Store all retrieved documents in a shared vector store and let Agent C re-query as needed
options.B: Have Agent B output structured claim objects that include a source_id field, and require Agent C to preserve and surface those source_ids in its output
options.C: Instruct Agent C via system prompt to mention 'according to retrieved documents' before each claim
options.D: Log all agent inputs and outputs to an audit database and generate citations post-hoc from the logs

questions.explanation: Claim-source mapping requires that every factual claim carries its source identifier throughout the processing pipeline. By having Agent B emit structured claim objects with source_id fields and requiring Agent C to propagate those fields, the provenance chain is maintained by design rather than reconstructed after the fact.

whyWrongMap.A: Re-querying the vector store does not guarantee that Agent C retrieves the same document that Agent B used; it creates a potential attribution mismatch rather than a traceable chain.
whyWrongMap.C: A generic phrase like 'according to retrieved documents' is not claim-source mapping — it provides no granular link between a specific claim and its originating document.
whyWrongMap.D: Post-hoc citation generation from logs is fragile, requires additional processing, and can fail if log entries are incomplete or if Agent C paraphrases claims in ways that are hard to reconcile with source text.

questions.scenario: A legal research tool powered by Claude aggregates case law from three databases. Database X says a precedent was overturned in 2019, Database Y says it remains valid, and Database Z has no entry. The tool must surface this to the attorney user.
questions.text: Which response structure best satisfies the conflict annotation requirement?

options.A: Present the most recently updated database's position as the authoritative answer
options.B: Present a blended synthesis: 'The precedent's validity is uncertain'
options.C: Explicitly state: 'Database X indicates this precedent was overturned in 2019; Database Y indicates it remains valid; Database Z contains no record. Attorney verification is recommended.'
options.D: Return an error to the user because conflicting sources prevent a reliable answer

questions.explanation: Conflict annotation requires surfacing disagreements explicitly, attributing each position to its source. Option C names each source and its specific claim, making the conflict visible so the attorney can exercise professional judgment. This is superior to synthesizing away the conflict, picking a winner, or refusing to respond.

whyWrongMap.A: Selecting the most recently updated source silently resolves the conflict without informing the user that a conflict exists, violating conflict annotation principles.
whyWrongMap.B: 'The precedent's validity is uncertain' is a blended synthesis that hides the specific nature and source of the disagreement, making it harder for the attorney to investigate.
whyWrongMap.D: Returning an error is an overcorrection; the system has useful information from two sources that the attorney needs, and withholding it in the presence of conflict is more harmful than surfacing the conflict clearly.

questions.scenario: A five-step summarization pipeline reduces a 50-page technical report to a one-page executive summary. Each step is performed by a separate Claude call with no structured metadata passed between steps. At the final step, Claude is asked to add citations.
questions.text: Why will the citation step likely fail to produce accurate claim-source mappings?

options.A: Claude cannot generate citations without access to the internet
options.B: The intermediate summaries have already stripped the granular source context needed to map specific claims back to original sections, causing provenance loss
options.C: The final Claude call's context window is too small to hold the original 50-page document
options.D: Citations require structured data types that plain-text summarization cannot produce

questions.explanation: Provenance loss in summarization chains occurs when each step discards source metadata. By the time citations are requested, the intermediate summaries contain claims but no record of which original section or page they came from. Adding citations at the end cannot recover information that was never passed through the pipeline.

whyWrongMap.A: Citation generation does not require internet access; the limitation here is the absence of preserved source metadata within the pipeline, not network connectivity.
whyWrongMap.C: Context window size is a real concern for very large documents, but the architectural flaw described is provenance loss — even with a large context window, unstructured summaries lack the source-mapping metadata needed for accurate attribution.
whyWrongMap.D: Citations can be generated from plain-text pipelines if source identifiers are explicitly tracked; the problem is that this pipeline does not track them, not that plain text is structurally incapable of carrying provenance.

questions.scenario: A Claude-based news aggregator pulls articles from 15 sources daily. Some articles were published hours ago; others are three days old. The system produces a single 'current events' brief without noting article publication dates.
questions.text: Which combination of provenance practices is most critical to add to this system?

options.A: Conflict annotation and uncertainty expression
options.B: Temporal data handling and claim-source mapping
options.C: Citation tracking across synthesis steps and conflict annotation
options.D: Provenance loss prevention and uncertainty expression

questions.explanation: The scenario has two primary gaps: (1) no publication dates are surfaced (temporal data handling), meaning users cannot assess whether information is still current; and (2) claims are not linked to their originating articles (claim-source mapping), preventing verification. These two practices together address the identified deficiencies.

whyWrongMap.A: Conflict annotation and uncertainty expression are valuable but secondary here — the scenario does not describe inter-source disagreements, and the primary structural gaps are temporal and attribution-related.
whyWrongMap.C: Citation tracking across synthesis steps is important in multi-step pipelines, but this scenario describes a single synthesis step; the bigger gaps are the absence of dates and source attribution on individual claims.
whyWrongMap.D: Provenance loss prevention is relevant but vague as a standalone practice; the scenario's specific gaps are temporal context and claim attribution, which are better addressed by the named practices in option B.

questions.scenario: A data analyst asks Claude to compare two analyst reports about the same company's revenue. Report A (Q1 2024) projects $500M annual revenue; Report B (Q3 2024) projects $420M. Claude responds: 'Analysts project annual revenue of approximately $460M.'
questions.text: Which two provenance principles does this response violate?

options.A: Claim-source mapping and temporal data handling
options.B: Conflict annotation and uncertainty expression
options.C: Citation tracking and provenance loss prevention
options.D: Temporal data handling and citation tracking

questions.explanation: Claude averaged two divergent figures into a single number without disclosing the discrepancy (conflict annotation violation) or attributing each projection to its source with qualifying language like 'Report A says X, Report B says Y' (uncertainty expression violation). The averaging synthesizes away meaningful disagreement between the two reports.

whyWrongMap.A: Claim-source mapping concerns the general linking of claims to sources, and temporal data handling concerns staleness flags — while relevant, the more specific violations here are conflict annotation (hiding the disagreement) and uncertainty expression (not naming each source's position).
whyWrongMap.C: Citation tracking and provenance loss prevention are pipeline-level concerns; the response is a single synthesis step, and the primary failure is hiding a conflict rather than a structural pipeline issue.
whyWrongMap.D: Temporal data handling is partially relevant (the reports are from different quarters), but it is not the primary violation — the core problem is that the disagreement between the two reports is hidden by averaging.

questions.scenario: An architect is designing a high-stakes medical information system. The system uses a four-agent pipeline: (1) Retrieval Agent fetches PubMed abstracts, (2) Extraction Agent pulls specific claims with their PMID identifiers, (3) Synthesis Agent merges claims into a clinical summary, (4) Formatting Agent produces the final output for clinicians. The team discovers that the Synthesis Agent's prompt does not require it to preserve PMID identifiers when merging claims.
questions.text: What is the most architecturally rigorous fix, and why does it matter more in a medical context than a general one?

options.A: Add a post-processing step that re-queries PubMed to reconstruct citations from the final summary text, because medical information requires verified citations
options.B: Redefine the Synthesis Agent's output schema to require a structured claims array where each claim object mandates a source_pmids field, and validate this schema before passing output to the Formatting Agent — because in medical contexts a missing or incorrect citation can directly contribute to patient harm
options.C: Instruct the Formatting Agent to add a footer stating 'Based on PubMed research' to satisfy citation requirements for clinical use
options.D: Replace the four-agent pipeline with a single large-context Claude call that receives all abstracts at once, eliminating the inter-agent handoff where provenance is lost

questions.explanation: The architecturally rigorous fix is to enforce provenance at the data model level — requiring a source_pmids field in every claim object ensures that provenance cannot be accidentally dropped during synthesis. Schema validation before the next agent receives the data creates a hard contract. In medical contexts this matters more because incorrect or missing attribution can lead clinicians to misapply evidence, with direct patient safety consequences rather than merely informational ones.

whyWrongMap.A: Post-hoc citation reconstruction from summary text is unreliable: the Synthesis Agent may paraphrase claims in ways that cannot be matched back to their original abstract, and the reconstruction step itself can introduce new errors — particularly dangerous in medical contexts.
whyWrongMap.C: A generic footer attribution provides no granular claim-source mapping; it does not tell clinicians which specific claim comes from which study, and it does not satisfy the standard for evidence-based clinical information.
whyWrongMap.D: A single large-context call may help with context coherence but does not structurally enforce claim-source mapping — the model could still produce a summary that blends sources without attribution, and it trades the provenance problem for a scalability and auditability problem.

questions.scenario: A financial intelligence platform builds a daily briefing using a three-step pipeline. Step 1 collects 200 news articles (with publication timestamps). Step 2 runs a Claude summarization that produces 20 topic summaries but discards publication timestamps. Step 3 runs a second Claude call that merges the 20 topic summaries into a single briefing. A compliance officer asks: 'Can you tell me which claim in the briefing comes from an article older than 48 hours?' The engineering team cannot answer.
questions.text: Which root cause analysis most accurately explains this failure, and what single architectural change would prevent it?

options.A: Root cause: Step 3 context window is insufficient. Fix: Increase token limit for Step 3.
options.B: Root cause: The pipeline lacks an audit log. Fix: Log all Claude inputs and outputs to a database.
options.C: Root cause: Publication timestamps are discarded in Step 2, causing provenance loss that propagates forward and makes temporal tracing impossible. Fix: Require Step 2 to output structured topic summaries that include a sources array, where each source entry carries its publication_timestamp.
options.D: Root cause: Claude summarization inherently loses metadata. Fix: Replace Claude with a deterministic extraction algorithm that preserves all metadata fields.

questions.explanation: The root cause is architectural: Step 2 receives articles with timestamps but its output schema does not carry those timestamps forward. Once discarded, the timestamps cannot be reconstructed downstream. The fix is to enforce provenance preservation at the data model level in Step 2 — structured outputs with a sources array and publication_timestamp ensure that every subsequent step has the temporal metadata it needs to answer compliance questions.

whyWrongMap.A: Context window size is unrelated to this failure; the problem is that timestamps were discarded by design in Step 2, not that they were present but could not fit in Step 3.
whyWrongMap.B: An audit log captures inputs and outputs but does not, by itself, link specific claims in the final briefing back to specific source articles — it would require complex matching logic and would still fail if Step 2 paraphrased claims beyond recognition.
whyWrongMap.D: Replacing Claude with a deterministic algorithm is an extreme overreaction; the failure is not inherent to LLM summarization but to an output schema that does not mandate metadata preservation. LLM summarization with a structured output requirement can preserve provenance.

questions.scenario: A policy research assistant synthesizes 12 government reports on climate adaptation spending. Three reports (from 2019, 2021, 2023) use different methodologies to measure 'adaptation spending,' making their figures not directly comparable. Two of the 12 reports are from the same country but give contradictory totals for 2022. The remaining seven reports are consistent. The assistant produces a single paragraph stating an aggregate global figure.
questions.text: Which provenance and uncertainty handling strategy is most appropriate for this synthesis?

options.A: Average all figures to produce a single defensible aggregate, then add a footnote: 'Figures may vary due to differing methodologies'
options.B: Exclude the three methodologically inconsistent reports and the two conflicting national reports from the synthesis, and base the output only on the seven consistent reports
options.C: Produce the synthesis in a structured format that: (i) presents the seven consistent reports' aggregate with source citations; (ii) explicitly notes the three reports use non-comparable methodologies with their specific methodological differences described; (iii) presents both contradictory national totals side-by-side with their source dates; and (iv) recommends expert review before policy use
options.D: Present the most recent report's figures as definitive, since newer data supersedes older data on the same topic

questions.explanation: This scenario requires three distinct provenance practices simultaneously: claim-source mapping (citing the seven consistent reports), conflict annotation (surfacing the contradictory national totals), and uncertainty expression (describing the methodological incompatibilities rather than hiding them). Option C is the only choice that addresses all three dimensions without silently discarding or blending conflicting information. The recommendation for expert review appropriately flags the synthesis as insufficient for high-stakes policy decisions.

whyWrongMap.A: Averaging figures with incompatible methodologies produces a misleading number; a footnote about 'differing methodologies' does not constitute the specific conflict annotation and uncertainty expression required — it is a generic disclaimer rather than a substantive disclosure.
whyWrongMap.B: Silently excluding 5 of 12 reports without informing the user constitutes provenance loss and hides relevant (even if problematic) information; the user cannot make an informed judgment about the exclusions if they are unaware of them.
whyWrongMap.D: Recency does not resolve methodological incompatibility or factual contradiction; 'the newest report' may use a narrower definition of spending than older reports, making a direct comparison invalid regardless of date.

questions.scenario: An architect is evaluating two designs for a multi-hop research agent. Design X: each agent call passes only the synthesized text to the next agent, with source metadata stored in a side-channel database that can be queried by request ID. Design Y: each agent call passes both synthesized text and a structured provenance_graph object containing all source claims, their identifiers, timestamps, and any flagged conflicts. Design X is 40% faster and uses 60% less token budget per call.
questions.text: Under which conditions should an architect choose Design X over Design Y despite its provenance tradeoffs?

options.A: When the downstream task is exploratory research where the user wants a quick orientation and will independently verify claims before acting on them, and the system explicitly informs users of the side-channel provenance model
options.B: When the system is used for compliance reporting, because the database lookup provides auditable records that satisfy regulatory requirements
options.C: When the synthesized text is short enough that the model is unlikely to hallucinate claims
options.D: When all source documents are from the same publisher, eliminating the need for conflict annotation

questions.explanation: Design X's tradeoff is acceptable when: (1) the use case is low-stakes exploratory orientation rather than authoritative output, (2) users are explicitly informed that they must independently verify claims, and (3) the side-channel database is accessible and functional. In this narrow context, the performance gains are worth the reduced inline provenance. For any high-stakes or terminal-output use case, Design Y's inline provenance graph is necessary because side-channel lookups can fail, be overlooked, or create a usability friction that means users never actually check.

whyWrongMap.B: Compliance reporting requires provenance to be tightly coupled to claims in the output — a separate database lookup creates a chain-of-custody gap; if the database is queried separately from the report, it is easier to dispute the linkage between a specific claim and its source.
whyWrongMap.C: Response length does not reliably predict hallucination risk, and even short summaries can introduce errors when merging sources; provenance is needed regardless of output length to verify accuracy.
whyWrongMap.D: Same-publisher sources can still contradict each other across time periods or due to data revisions; eliminating conflict annotation based on publisher identity is an unreliable heuristic that violates the conflict annotation principle.
